name: distributions

members.name: Bernoulli
members.signature: Bernoulli(probs: Tensor | float | None = None, logits: Tensor | float | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(probs: Tensor | float | None = None, logits: Tensor | float | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Bernoulli distribution.
methods.extended:
Exactly one of `probs` or `logits` must be supplied.  The other
is derived on demand via the sigmoid / logit transform, so there is no
redundant storage.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Success probability $p \in [0, 1]$.  Mutually exclusive
with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds $\ell = \log(p / (1-p)) \in \mathbb{R}$.
Mutually exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

raises.description: If both or neither of `probs` / `logits` are provided.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Bernoulli
>>> d = Bernoulli(probs=0.7)
>>> d.mean
Tensor(0.7)
>>> d2 = Bernoulli(logits=0.0)  # p = 0.5
>>> d2.probs  # derived lazily
Tensor(0.5)

methods.name: param
methods.summary: The canonical parameter used at construction time.
methods.extended:
Returns the logits tensor when the distribution was constructed with
`logits`, or the probs tensor when constructed with `probs`.
This is used by `ExponentialFamily` machinery to access the
sufficient statistic parameter without forcing a conversion.

returns.description:
Either `self.logits` or `self.probs`, depending on which was
provided at construction.

examples:
>>> d = Bernoulli(probs=0.3)
>>> d.param  # returns self.probs
Tensor(0.3)

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Expected value of the distribution.
methods.extended:
For a Bernoulli random variable $X \in \{0, 1\}$ with
success probability $p$:

$$
E[X] = p
$$
examples:
>>> Bernoulli(probs=0.3).mean
Tensor(0.3)

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the distribution.
methods.extended:
For a Bernoulli random variable $X$ with success probability
$p$:

$$
\operatorname{Var}[X] = p(1 - p)
$$

The variance is maximised at $p = 0.5$ and equals zero at the
degenerate extremes $p \in \{0, 1\}$.
examples:
>>> Bernoulli(probs=0.5).variance
Tensor(0.25)

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the Bernoulli distribution.
methods.extended:
Samples by comparing uniform noise $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$
against the success probability $p$:

$$
X = \mathbf{1}[U < p]
$$

The returned tensor has integer-valued entries in $\{0, 1\}$
stored as floating-point and is **detached** from the autograd graph.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description:
Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  The full output
shape is `sample_shape + batch_shape`.  Default is `()`.

examples:
>>> d = Bernoulli(probs=0.6)
>>> x = d.sample((1000,))
>>> x.mean()  # approximately 0.6

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability of `value` under the Bernoulli distribution.
methods.extended:
Uses the numerically stable logits form to avoid $\log(0)$:

$$
\log p(x \mid \ell) = x \cdot \ell - \log(1 + e^\ell)
$$

where $\ell = \log(p / (1-p))$ is the log-odds.  This is
equivalent to the cross-entropy form
$x \log p + (1-x) \log(1-p)$ but avoids numerical issues at
the boundaries $p \in \{0, 1\}$.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Observed values.  Should be in $\{0, 1\}$.

examples:
>>> d = Bernoulli(probs=0.7)
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(1.0))  # log(0.7)
Tensor(-0.3567)

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Bernoulli distribution (in nats).
methods.extended:
$$
H(X) = -p \log p - (1-p) \log(1-p)
$$

Computed in the numerically stable softplus form:

$$
H = \log(1 + e^\ell) - p \cdot \ell
$$

where $\ell$ is the log-odds.  The entropy is maximised at
$p = 0.5$ (maximum uncertainty) and is zero at the degenerate
cases $p \in \{0, 1\}$.
examples:
>>> Bernoulli(probs=0.5).entropy()  # log(2) ≈ 0.693
Tensor(0.6931)

members.summary: Bernoulli distribution over $\{0, 1\}$.
members.extended:
The Bernoulli is the simplest discrete distribution: a single coin
flip with success probability $p \in [0, 1]$.  It models a binary
outcome and is the building block of the Binomial (sum of `n` IID
Bernoullis), the Categorical (its multinomial generalisation), and most
classification likelihoods in supervised learning.

Specify *exactly one* of `probs` or `logits`; the other is derived
lazily via the sigmoid / logit transform so there is no redundant
storage and the parameterisation chosen at construction remains the
canonical one for autograd.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Success probability $p \in [0, 1]$.  Mutually exclusive
with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds $\ell = \log(p / (1 - p)) \in \mathbb{R}$.
Mutually exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Bernoulli
>>> d = Bernoulli(probs=0.7)
>>> d.mean
Tensor(0.7)
>>> d.sample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(1.0))
Tensor(-0.3567)
notes:
Probability mass function on $x \in \{0, 1\}$:



$$
P(X = k \mid p) = p^k (1 - p)^{1-k}
$$

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = p, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = p(1 - p), \qquad
H[X] = -p\log p - (1-p)\log(1-p)
$$

The variance is maximised at $p = 0.5$ (maximum uncertainty) and
vanishes at the degenerate endpoints $p \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Relation to other distributions:**

* $\mathrm{Binomial}(n, p)$ is the sum of $n$ independent
  $\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)$ draws.
* $\mathrm{Geometric}(p)$ counts Bernoulli failures before the
  first success.
* $\mathrm{Categorical}$ generalises Bernoulli to $K > 2$
  categories.

Conjugate prior: `lucid.distributions.Beta` — observing
$k$ successes out of $n$ updates
`Beta(α, β) → Beta(α + k, β + n - k)`.

members.name: Geometric
members.signature: Geometric(probs: Tensor | float | None = None, logits: Tensor | float | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(probs: Tensor | float | None = None, logits: Tensor | float | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Geometric distribution.
methods.extended:
Exactly one of `probs` or `logits` must be provided.  If
`logits` is given, it is converted to `probs` via the sigmoid
transform and stored as `self.probs`.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Success probability $p \in (0, 1)$ of a single Bernoulli
trial.  Mutually exclusive with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds $\ell = \log(p / (1-p))$.  Converted to
`probs` at construction time.  Mutually exclusive with
`probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

raises.description: If both or neither of `probs` / `logits` are provided.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Geometric
>>> d = Geometric(probs=0.25)
>>> d.mean  # E[X] = (1-p)/p = 3
Tensor(3.0)
notes:
The support is $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ representing the number
of *failures* before the first success.  The PMF is:



$$
P(X = k) = (1 - p)^k \, p, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Expected number of failures before the first success.
examples:
>>> Geometric(probs=0.5).mean
Tensor(1.0)

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the number of failures before the first success.
examples:
>>> Geometric(probs=0.5).variance
Tensor(2.0)

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the Geometric distribution.
methods.extended:
Uses the inverse-CDF trick: if $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$,

$$
X = \left\lfloor \frac{\log U}{\log(1 - p)} \right\rfloor
$$

A small epsilon clamp guards against $U = 0$ which would
produce $-\infty$.  The returned tensor is **detached** since
the Geometric is discrete.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description:
Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  The full output
shape is `sample_shape + batch_shape`.  Default is `()`.

returns.description: Non-negative integer samples of shape `sample_shape + batch_shape`.

examples:
>>> d = Geometric(probs=0.5)
>>> x = d.sample((1000,))
>>> x.mean()  # approximately 1.0

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability of `value` under the Geometric distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log P(X = k) = k \log(1-p) + \log p
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Non-negative integer outcomes $k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$.

examples:
>>> d = Geometric(probs=0.5)
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.0))  # log(0.5) ≈ -0.693
Tensor(-0.6931)

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Geometric distribution (in nats).
methods.extended:
$$
H(X) = \frac{-(1-p)\log(1-p) - p \log p}{p}
$$

The entropy grows without bound as $p \to 0$ (more uncertainty
over many possible outcomes) and is zero at $p = 1$ (certain
success on first trial).
examples:
>>> Geometric(probs=0.5).entropy()
Tensor(1.3863)

members.summary: Geometric distribution: number of failures before the first success.
members.extended:
Models the number of failed Bernoulli trials $X \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$
preceding the first success in an unbounded sequence of independent
trials with success probability $p$.  This is the *shifted*
convention (counting failures); the alternative convention counts trials
including the success and starts at $1$.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Success probability $p \in (0, 1)$ per trial.  Mutually
exclusive with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds $\ell = \log(p/(1-p))$.  Converted to `probs` via
the sigmoid at construction.  Mutually exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Geometric
>>> d = Geometric(probs=0.25)
>>> d.mean  # (1 - p)/p = 3.0
Tensor(3.0)
>>> d.sample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(2.0))
Tensor(...)
notes:
Probability mass function:



$$
P(X = k \mid p) = p (1 - p)^k, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots
$$

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1 - p}{p}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = \frac{1 - p}{p^2}
$$

**Memoryless property** — the Geometric is the *only* discrete
distribution with the memoryless property:



$$
P(X \geq m + n \mid X \geq m) = P(X \geq n)
$$

Continuous analogue: `lucid.distributions.Exponential`
(the only continuous memoryless distribution).

The expected number of *trials* (including the success) is
$1/p$; entropy grows without bound as $p \to 0$ and is
zero at $p = 1$.

members.name: Categorical
members.signature: Categorical(probs: Tensor | None = None, logits: Tensor | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(probs: Tensor | None = None, logits: Tensor | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Categorical distribution.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Non-negative probability vector of shape `(..., K)`.  Rows are
automatically normalised to sum to 1.  Mutually exclusive with
`logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Unnormalised log-probabilities of shape `(..., K)`.  Converted
to probabilities via softmax internally.  Mutually exclusive with
`probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

raises.description: If both or neither of `probs` and `logits` are provided.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: Support of the distribution: integer interval $\{0, \ldots, K-1\}$.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the Categorical distribution (undefined — returns NaN).
methods.extended:
The Categorical distribution assigns labels with no inherent ordering
or metric, so the mean is not well-defined.  This property returns a
`NaN` tensor of the batch shape to match expected behaviour.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the Categorical distribution.
methods.extended:
Uses the **Gumbel-max trick**: add i.i.d.
$\operatorname{Gumbel}(0, 1)$ noise to the log-probabilities and
take the argmax, which is equivalent to ancestral sampling but avoids
cumulative-sum and binary search.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

returns.description:
Integer tensor of shape `(*sample_shape, *batch_shape)` with
values in $\{0, 1, \ldots, K-1\}$.  The result is detached
(no gradients flow through discrete samples).

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability of the given category indices.
methods.extended:
For a one-hot index $k$, the log-probability is:

$$
\log P(X = k) = \log p_k
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description:
Integer tensor of category indices with shape compatible with
`batch_shape`.  Values must be in $\{0, \ldots, K-1\}$.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Categorical distribution.

members.summary: Categorical distribution — a discrete distribution over K labelled outcomes.
members.extended:
`Categorical(probs=p)` or `Categorical(logits=l)` defines a distribution
over the integer set $\{0, 1, \ldots, K-1\}$ where $K$ is the
number of categories.  Exactly one of `probs` or `logits` must be given.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Non-negative probability vector (or batch of vectors) of shape
`(..., K)`.  Rows are automatically normalised to sum to 1.
Mutually exclusive with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Unnormalised log-probabilities of shape `(..., K)`.
The distribution uses $\text{softmax}$ internally to convert to
normalised probabilities.  Mutually exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Categorical
>>> # Uniform over 4 categories
>>> dist = Categorical(probs=lucid.tensor([0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]))
>>> samples = dist.sample((10,))
>>> # Batch of 2 distributions
>>> dist_b = Categorical(logits=lucid.zeros(2, 5))
>>> dist_b.batch_shape, dist_b.event_shape
((2,), ())
notes:
**PMF**:



$$
P(X = k) = p_k, \quad k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, K-1\},
\quad \sum_{k} p_k = 1
$$

**Parameterisations** are related by:



$$
p_k = \frac{e^{l_k}}{\sum_j e^{l_j}}, \qquad
l_k = \log p_k \;(\text{up to additive constant})
$$

**Entropy**:



$$
H[X] = -\sum_{k=0}^{K-1} p_k \log p_k
$$

**Mean** is not well-defined for a general Categorical (the labels have no
canonical metric), so `mean` returns a `NaN` tensor of the batch shape.

**Sampling** uses the **Gumbel-max trick**: add i.i.d.
$\operatorname{Gumbel}(0, 1)$ noise to the log-probabilities and
take the argmax.  This is equivalent to ancestral sampling and avoids
cumulative-sum + binary-search.

The batch dimensions of the input correspond to independent distributions.
For example, `probs` of shape `(B, K)` yields a batch of $B$
Categorical distributions.

attributes.name: probs
attributes.description:
Normalised probability vector (shape `(..., K)`; present when
constructed with `probs`).

attributes.name: logits
attributes.description:
Unnormalised log-probability vector (shape `(..., K)`; present when
constructed with `logits`).

members.name: OneHotCategorical
members.signature: OneHotCategorical(probs: Tensor | None = None, logits: Tensor | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(probs: Tensor | None = None, logits: Tensor | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a OneHotCategorical distribution.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Non-negative probability vector of shape `(..., K)`.  Normalised
internally.  Mutually exclusive with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Unnormalised log-probabilities of shape `(..., K)`.  Mutually
exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

raises.description: If both or neither of `probs` and `logits` are provided.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: Support of the distribution: the probability simplex.

returns.description:
The `simplex` constraint, as each sample is a one-hot vector
whose entries are non-negative and sum to 1.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw one-hot encoded samples.
methods.extended:
Internally samples category indices from the underlying
`Categorical` distribution and converts them to one-hot
vectors via `one_hot`.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

returns.description:
Float tensor of shape `(*sample_shape, *batch_shape, K)`
containing one-hot vectors (exactly one 1 per row).

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability of a one-hot encoded sample.
methods.extended:
For a one-hot vector $e_k$ (all zeros except a 1 at position
$k$):

$$
\log P(X = e_k) = \sum_j e_{kj} \log p_j = \log p_k
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: One-hot tensor of shape `(..., K)` with `float` dtype.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the OneHotCategorical distribution.
methods.extended:
Equal to the entropy of the underlying `Categorical`:

$$
H[X] = -\sum_{k=0}^{K-1} p_k \log p_k
$$

members.summary: Categorical distribution with one-hot encoded samples.
members.extended:
`OneHotCategorical` wraps a `Categorical` and returns samples
as one-hot vectors of shape `(..., K)` instead of integer indices.
It is particularly useful for:

- **REINFORCE-style gradient estimators** where you need a discrete
  sample but want to use it in differentiable downstream computation.
- **Relaxations** — replacing with `RelaxedOneHotCategorical`
  gives a differentiable approximation that converges to one-hot as
  temperature $\to 0$.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Non-negative probability vector `(..., K)`.  Normalised internally.
Mutually exclusive with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Unnormalised log-probabilities `(..., K)`.  Mutually exclusive with
`probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import OneHotCategorical
>>> dist = OneHotCategorical(probs=lucid.tensor([0.1, 0.5, 0.4]))
>>> sample = dist.sample()
>>> sample.shape  # (3,) — one-hot
(3,)
>>> sample.sum()  # always 1
notes:
**Samples** are integer-valued one-hot vectors in $\{0, 1\}^K$
with exactly one 1 at the sampled category index.

**Log-probability** for a one-hot vector $e_k$:



$$
\log P(X = e_k) = \sum_{j} e_{kj} \log p_j = \log p_k
$$

which is simply the log-probability of the selected category.

**Entropy** equals that of the underlying `Categorical`:



$$
H[X] = -\sum_{k} p_k \log p_k
$$

The `event_shape` is `(K,)` whereas `Categorical` has
`event_shape = ()`.

attributes.name: probs
attributes.description: Normalised probability vector (present when constructed with `probs`).

attributes.name: logits
attributes.description:
Unnormalised log-probability vector (present when constructed with
`logits`).

members.name: FisherSnedecor
members.signature: FisherSnedecor(df1: Tensor | float, df2: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(df1: Tensor | float, df2: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Fisher-Snedecor F-distribution.

parameters.name: df1
parameters.description: Numerator degrees of freedom $d_1 > 0$.

parameters.name: df2
parameters.description: Denominator degrees of freedom $d_2 > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the F-distribution: $E[X] = d_2 / (d_2 - 2)$.
methods.extended: Defined only for $d_2 > 2$; returns `inf` or `nan` otherwise.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the F-distribution.
methods.extended:
$\operatorname{Var}[X] = 2 d_2^2 (d_1 + d_2 - 2) / [d_1 (d_2 - 2)^2 (d_2 - 4)]$

Defined only for $d_2 > 4$.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the F-distribution.
methods.extended:
Forms the ratio $F = (X/d_1) / (Y/d_2)$ where
$X \sim \chi^2(d_1)$ and $Y \sim \chi^2(d_2)$ are
independent samples drawn via the Gamma sampler.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the F-distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x) = \frac{d_1}{2}\log(d_1 x)
           + \frac{d_2}{2}\log d_2
           - \frac{d_1+d_2}{2}\log(d_1 x + d_2)
           - \log x - \log B(d_1/2, d_2/2)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Positive points $x > 0$ at which to evaluate the density.

returns.description: Log-density values of the same shape as `value`.

members.summary: Fisher-Snedecor F-distribution — ratio of two scaled Chi-squared variates.
members.extended:
`FisherSnedecor(df1=d1, df2=d2)` is the distribution of the statistic

$$
F = \frac{X / d_1}{Y / d_2}
$$

where $X \sim \chi^2(d_1)$ and $Y \sim \chi^2(d_2)$ are
independent.  The F-distribution is foundational in classical statistics:
it arises in ANOVA F-tests and in the likelihood-ratio test for comparing
nested linear models.

parameters.name: df1
parameters.description: Numerator degrees of freedom $d_1 > 0$.

parameters.name: df2
parameters.description: Denominator degrees of freedom $d_2 > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import FisherSnedecor
>>> dist = FisherSnedecor(df1=5.0, df2=10.0)
>>> samples = dist.sample((100,))
>>> dist.mean  # d2/(d2-2) = 10/8 = 1.25
notes:
**PDF**:



$$
p(x; d_1, d_2) = \frac{1}{x \, B(d_1/2,\, d_2/2)}
\left(\frac{d_1 x}{d_1 x + d_2}\right)^{d_1/2}
\left(\frac{d_2}{d_1 x + d_2}\right)^{d_2/2},
\quad x > 0
$$

**Log-PDF** (stable form using lgamma):



$$
\log p(x) = \frac{d_1}{2}\log(d_1 x)
           + \frac{d_2}{2}\log d_2
           - \frac{d_1+d_2}{2}\log(d_1 x + d_2)
           - \log x - \log B(d_1/2, d_2/2)
$$

**Moments** (when defined):

- Mean ($d_2 > 2$): $E[X] = d_2 / (d_2 - 2)$
- Variance ($d_2 > 4$):
  $\operatorname{Var}[X] = 2 d_2^2 (d_1 + d_2 - 2) / [d_1 (d_2 - 2)^2 (d_2 - 4)]$

**Sampling** draws one $\chi^2(d_1)$ and one $\chi^2(d_2)$
sample and forms the ratio.  Both $\chi^2$ samples are drawn via
the Gamma sampler.

members.name: HalfCauchy

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(scale: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a HalfCauchy distribution.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description:
Scale parameter $s > 0$.  The half-width at half-maximum
of the Cauchy density folded onto $[0, \infty)$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Draw reparameterised samples by folding a Cauchy sample.
methods.extended:
Computes `abs` of a zero-location `Cauchy` sample, so
gradients propagate through the scale parameter.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the HalfCauchy distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x) = \log 2 + \log\operatorname{Cauchy}(x; 0, s)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Non-negative points $x \geq 0$ at which to evaluate.

returns.description: Log-density values of the same shape as `value`.

members.summary: Half-Cauchy distribution — the absolute value of a zero-location Cauchy.
members.extended:
If $X \sim \operatorname{Cauchy}(0, s)$ then
$|X| \sim \operatorname{HalfCauchy}(s)$.
The distribution is supported on $[0, \infty)$ and has **no finite
moments** (mean and variance are undefined, like the full Cauchy).

It is a popular weakly-informative prior for scale parameters in Bayesian
hierarchical models — heavy tails allow occasional large scales while the
mode at zero permits near-zero scales.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description:
Scale parameter $s > 0$.  This is the half-width at half-maximum
of the Cauchy density folded onto $[0, \infty)$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import HalfCauchy
>>> dist = HalfCauchy(scale=1.0)
>>> samples = dist.rsample((200,))
>>> (samples >= 0.0).all()
notes:
**PDF**:



$$
p(x; s) = \frac{2}{\pi s \left(1 + (x/s)^2\right)},
\quad x \geq 0
$$

**Log-PDF**:



$$
\log p(x) = \log 2 + \log\operatorname{Cauchy}(x; 0, s)
$$

where the right-hand side is the log-density of the full Cauchy evaluated
at $x$.

Neither the mean nor the variance exists because the Cauchy distribution
lacks finite moments of any positive order.

**Reparameterised sampling** folds a Cauchy sample via `abs`, so gradients
propagate through the scale parameter.

members.summary: Half-Normal distribution — the absolute value of a zero-mean Normal.
members.extended:
If $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ then
$|X| \sim \operatorname{HalfNormal}(\sigma)$.
The distribution is supported on $[0, \infty)$ and arises
naturally as a scale prior in hierarchical Bayesian models (it is
weakly informative while keeping probability mass away from zero).

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description:
Scale parameter $\sigma > 0$ — the standard deviation of
the underlying zero-mean Normal.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import HalfNormal
>>> dist = HalfNormal(scale=1.0)
>>> samples = dist.rsample((300,))
>>> (samples >= 0.0).all()
notes:
**PDF**:



$$
p(x; \sigma) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sigma\sqrt{\pi}}
\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right),
\quad x \geq 0
$$

**Log-PDF**:



$$
\log p(x) = \log 2 + \log\mathcal{N}(x; 0, \sigma^2)
$$

where the right-hand side is the log-density of the full Normal evaluated
at $x$.

**Moments**:

- Mean: $E[X] = \sigma\sqrt{2/\pi}$
- Variance: $\operatorname{Var}[X] = \sigma^2(1 - 2/\pi)$

**Entropy**:



$$
H[X] = \tfrac{1}{2}\log\!\left(\frac{\pi e \sigma^2}{2}\right)
$$

**Reparameterised sampling** takes `abs` of a Normal sample, so
gradients flow through $\sigma$ unobstructed.

members.name: Pareto
members.signature: Pareto(scale: Tensor | float, alpha: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(scale: Tensor | float, alpha: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Pareto distribution.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description:
Scale parameter $x_m > 0$ — the minimum value of the
support (lower bound of the distribution).

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Shape (tail-index) parameter $\alpha > 0$.  Smaller
values produce heavier tails.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: Support of the Pareto distribution: positive reals $(0, \infty)$.
methods.extended:
The true support is $[x_m, \infty)$ per element, but since the
lower bound varies across the batch a bare `positive` constraint is
returned.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the Pareto distribution: $E[X] = \alpha x_m / (\alpha - 1)$.
methods.extended:
Defined only for $\alpha > 1$; returns `inf` or `nan`
for $\alpha \leq 1$.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Pareto distribution.
methods.extended:
$\operatorname{Var}[X] = x_m^2 \alpha / ((\alpha-1)^2 (\alpha-2))$

Defined only for $\alpha > 2$.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Draw reparameterised samples via the inverse-CDF trick.
methods.extended:
Uses $X = x_m (1 - U)^{-1/\alpha}$ where
$U \sim \operatorname{Uniform}(0, 1)$.  Gradients propagate
through both `scale` and `alpha`.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the Pareto distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x) = \log\alpha + \alpha\log x_m - (\alpha+1)\log x
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Points $x \geq x_m$ at which to evaluate the density.

returns.description: Log-density values of the same shape as `value`.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Entropy of the Pareto distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
H[X] = \log(x_m / \alpha) + 1/\alpha + 1
$$

members.summary: Pareto distribution — a heavy-tailed power-law family on $[x_m, \infty)$.
members.extended:
`Pareto(scale=x_m, alpha=α)` models quantities that follow a power-law
tail.  The 80/20 rule (Pareto principle) is a heuristic consequence of
this distribution with $\alpha \approx 1.16$.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description:
Scale parameter $x_m > 0$ — the minimum possible value of
the random variable (the lower bound of the support).

parameters.name: alpha
validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Pareto
>>> dist = Pareto(scale=1.0, alpha=2.0)
>>> samples = dist.rsample((200,))
>>> samples.min()  # always >= scale
notes:
**PDF**:



$$
p(x; x_m, \alpha) = \frac{\alpha \, x_m^\alpha}{x^{\alpha+1}},
\quad x \geq x_m
$$

**Log-PDF**:



$$
\log p(x) = \log\alpha + \alpha\log x_m - (\alpha+1)\log x
$$

**Moments** (when defined):

- Mean ($\alpha > 1$): $E[X] = \alpha x_m / (\alpha - 1)$
- Variance ($\alpha > 2$):
  $\operatorname{Var}[X] = x_m^2 \alpha / ((\alpha-1)^2 (\alpha-2))$

**Entropy**:



$$
H[X] = \log(x_m / \alpha) + 1/\alpha + 1
$$

**Reparameterised sampling** uses the inverse-CDF trick:
if $U \sim \operatorname{Uniform}(0, 1)$ then
$X = x_m (1-U)^{-1/\alpha} \sim \operatorname{Pareto}(x_m, \alpha)$.

members.name: Weibull
members.signature: Weibull(scale: Tensor | float, concentration: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(scale: Tensor | float, concentration: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Weibull distribution.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Scale parameter $\lambda > 0$.

parameters.name: concentration
validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: Support of the Weibull distribution: $[0, \infty)$.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the Weibull distribution: $E[X] = \lambda \Gamma(1 + 1/k)$.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Weibull distribution.
methods.extended: $\operatorname{Var}[X] = \lambda^2 [\Gamma(1+2/k) - \Gamma(1+1/k)^2]$

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Draw reparameterised samples via the inverse-CDF.
methods.extended:
Uses $X = \lambda (-\log(1-U))^{1/k}$ for
$U \sim \operatorname{Uniform}(0, 1)$.  Gradients propagate
through both `scale` and `concentration`.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the Weibull distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x) = \log k - k\log\lambda + (k-1)\log x - (x/\lambda)^k
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Non-negative points $x \geq 0$ at which to evaluate.

returns.description: Log-density values of the same shape as `value`.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Entropy of the Weibull distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
H[X] = \gamma(1 - 1/k) + \log(\lambda/k) + 1
$$

where $\gamma \approx 0.5772$ is the Euler-Mascheroni constant.

members.summary: Weibull distribution — a flexible family for lifetime and survival analysis.
members.extended:
`Weibull(scale=λ, concentration=k)` generalises both the Exponential
($k = 1$) and Rayleigh ($k = 2$) distributions.  It is widely
used for modelling time-to-failure data because the hazard rate
$h(t) = (k/\lambda)(t/\lambda)^{k-1}$ can increase, be constant,
or decrease depending on $k$.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description:
Scale parameter $\lambda > 0$ — a characteristic life value
at which the CDF is $1 - e^{-1} \approx 63.2\%$.

parameters.name: concentration
parameters.description:
Shape parameter $k > 0$.  Values $k < 1$ give a
decreasing hazard (infant mortality); $k = 1$ is constant
(memoryless Exponential); $k > 1$ gives an increasing hazard
(wear-out).

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Weibull
>>> # Exponential(rate=1) as a special case
>>> dist_exp = Weibull(scale=1.0, concentration=1.0)
>>> dist = Weibull(scale=2.0, concentration=1.5)
>>> samples = dist.rsample((500,))
notes:
**PDF**:



$$
p(x; \lambda, k) = \frac{k}{\lambda}
\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1}
\exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^k\right),
\quad x \geq 0
$$

**Log-PDF**:



$$
\log p(x) = \log k - k\log\lambda + (k-1)\log x
           - (x/\lambda)^k
$$

**Moments**:

- Mean: $E[X] = \lambda \,\Gamma(1 + 1/k)$
- Variance: $\operatorname{Var}[X] = \lambda^2 \left[\Gamma(1+2/k) - \Gamma(1+1/k)^2\right]$

**Entropy**:



$$
H[X] = \gamma\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{k}\right) + \log(\lambda/k) + 1
$$

where $\gamma \approx 0.5772$ is the Euler–Mascheroni constant.

**Reparameterised sampling** uses the inverse-CDF:
$X = \lambda(-\log(1-U))^{1/k}$ for $U \sim \operatorname{Uniform}(0,1)$.

members.name: Binomial
members.signature: Binomial(total_count: Tensor | int = 1, probs: Tensor | float | None = None, logits: Tensor | float | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(total_count: Tensor | int = 1, probs: Tensor | float | None = None, logits: Tensor | float | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Binomial distribution.

parameters.name: total_count
parameters.description:
Number of trials $n \geq 0$.  Default is `1` (reduces to
Bernoulli).

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Success probability $p \in [0, 1]$.  Mutually exclusive with
`logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds $l = \log(p / (1-p)) \in \mathbb{R}$.  Mutually
exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

raises.description: If both or neither of `probs` and `logits` are provided.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: Support of the Binomial distribution: non-negative integers.
methods.extended:
Although the strict support is $\{0, 1, \ldots, n\}$ per element,
this property returns `nonnegative_integer` because `total_count`
may differ across the batch.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the Binomial distribution: $E[X] = np$.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Binomial distribution: $\operatorname{Var}[X] = np(1-p)$.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the Binomial distribution.
methods.extended:
Uses an exact strategy for small $n$ (sum of Bernoulli draws)
and a Normal approximation with rounding for large $n$.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

returns.description:
Non-negative integer samples in $\{0, 1, \ldots, n\}$,
shape `(*sample_shape, *batch_shape)`.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability of the given counts under the Binomial distribution.
methods.extended:
Computed via logits for numerical stability:

$$
\log P(X = k) = \log\binom{n}{k} + k \, l - n \log(1 + e^l)
$$

where $l = \operatorname{logit}(p)$ and the binomial coefficient
is evaluated via $\log\Gamma$.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Count values $k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n\}$.

returns.description: Log-probability values of the same shape as `value`.

members.summary: Binomial distribution over the number of successes in n independent trials.
members.extended:
`Binomial(total_count=n, probs=p)` models the count of successes when
each of $n$ i.i.d. Bernoulli trials has success probability
$p$.  Parameterisation is via either `probs` (in $[0, 1]$)
or `logits` (the log-odds $\log(p/(1-p)) \in \mathbb{R}$);
exactly one must be supplied.

parameters.name: total_count
parameters.description:
Number of trials $n \geq 0$.  Default is `1` (reduces to
Bernoulli).

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Success probability $p \in [0, 1]$.  Mutually exclusive with
`logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds $l = \log(p / (1-p)) \in \mathbb{R}$.  Mutually
exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Binomial
>>> dist = Binomial(total_count=10, probs=0.3)
>>> samples = dist.sample((50,))
>>> samples.shape
(50,)
>>> # PMF at k=3
>>> dist.log_prob(lucid.tensor(3.0)).exp()
notes:
**PMF**:



$$
P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k},
\quad k \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n\}
$$

**Log-PMF** via logits (numerically stable form):



$$
\log P(X = k) = \log\binom{n}{k} + k \, l - n \log(1 + e^l)
$$

where $l = \operatorname{logit}(p)$ and the binomial coefficient is
evaluated via $\log \Gamma(n+1) - \log\Gamma(k+1) - \log\Gamma(n-k+1)$.

**Moments**:

- Mean: $E[X] = np$
- Variance: $\operatorname{Var}[X] = np(1-p)$

**Sampling strategy**: for $n \leq 25$ Bernoulli draws are summed
exactly along a dedicated axis.  For larger $n$ a Normal
approximation is used with the result rounded and clamped to
$[0, n]$.

members.name: NegativeBinomial
members.signature: NegativeBinomial(total_count: Tensor | float, probs: Tensor | float | None = None, logits: Tensor | float | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(total_count: Tensor | float, probs: Tensor | float | None = None, logits: Tensor | float | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Negative Binomial distribution.

parameters.name: total_count
parameters.description:
Dispersion / number of successes $r > 0$.  May be
non-integer for the generalised version.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Probability of failure $p \in (0, 1)$.  Mutually exclusive
with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds of failure $l = \log(p / (1-p))$.  Mutually
exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

raises.description: If both or neither of `probs` and `logits` are provided.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the Negative Binomial: $E[X] = r p / (1-p)$.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Negative Binomial: $\operatorname{Var}[X] = r p / (1-p)^2$.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples via the Gamma-Poisson compound representation.
methods.extended:
Draws $\lambda \sim \operatorname{Gamma}(r, (1-p)/p)$ then
$X \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda)$.  This produces exact
(non-approximate) samples for all real $r > 0$.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability of counts under the Negative Binomial distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log P(X = k) = \log\Gamma(k+r) - \log\Gamma(r) - \log\Gamma(k+1)
               + r \log(1-p) + k \log p
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Non-negative integer counts $k \geq 0$.

returns.description: Log-probability values of the same shape as `value`.

members.summary: Negative Binomial distribution over the number of failures before r successes.
members.extended:
`NegativeBinomial(total_count=r, probs=p)` models the count of failures
$k$ before achieving $r$ successes in a sequence of independent
Bernoulli trials each with success probability $1 - p$.

Equivalently, this is a **Gamma-Poisson compound**: draw
$\lambda \sim \operatorname{Gamma}(r, (1-p)/p)$, then
$X \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda)$.  This interpretation
extends the distribution to real-valued `total_count` (the
*generalised* Negative Binomial) and forms the basis of the sampler.

parameters.name: total_count
parameters.description:
Dispersion / number of successes $r > 0$.  May be non-integer
for the generalised version.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Probability of failure $p \in (0, 1)$.  Mutually exclusive
with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds of failure $l = \log(p / (1-p)) \in \mathbb{R}$.
Mutually exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import NegativeBinomial
>>> dist = NegativeBinomial(total_count=5.0, probs=0.4)
>>> samples = dist.sample((100,))
>>> samples.shape
(100,)
>>> dist.mean
notes:
**PMF**:



$$
P(X = k) = \binom{k + r - 1}{k} (1-p)^r p^k,
\quad k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}
$$

**Log-PMF** (via `lgamma` for numerical stability):



$$
\log P(X = k) = \log\Gamma(k+r) - \log\Gamma(r) - \log\Gamma(k+1)
               + r \log(1-p) + k \log p
$$

**Moments**:

- Mean: $E[X] = r p / (1-p)$
- Variance: $\operatorname{Var}[X] = r p / (1-p)^2$

**Sampler**: uses the Gamma-Poisson representation.  A standard-Gamma
variate with concentration $r$ is drawn via rejection sampling,
scaled by $p/(1-p)$, and passed to the Poisson sampler.  This
produces exact (non-approximate) samples for all real $r > 0$.

attributes.name: logits
attributes.description: Log-odds of failure (present when constructed with `logits`).

members.name: Poisson
members.signature: Poisson(rate: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(rate: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Poisson distribution.

parameters.name: rate
parameters.description:
Mean rate parameter $\lambda > 0$.  Determines both the
mean and the variance.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the Poisson distribution: $E[X] = \lambda$.

methods.name: mode
methods.summary: Mode of the Poisson distribution: $\lfloor \lambda \rfloor$.
methods.extended:
When $\lambda$ is an integer, both $\lambda$ and
$\lambda - 1$ are modes; this property returns
$\lfloor \lambda \rfloor$.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Poisson distribution: $\operatorname{Var}[X] = \lambda$.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the Poisson distribution.
methods.extended:
Delegates to `lucid.poisson`, which uses the Knuth exact
algorithm for small $\lambda$ and a Normal approximation
with rounding for large $\lambda$.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability of the given counts under the Poisson distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log P(X = k) = k \log \lambda - \lambda - \log\Gamma(k + 1)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Non-negative integer counts $k \geq 0$.

returns.description: Log-probability values of the same shape as `value`.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Entropy of the Poisson distribution (not available in closed form).
methods.extended:
The Poisson entropy is an infinite sum over all non-negative integers
and has no simple closed form.

raises.description: Always — use numerical approximation if needed.

members.summary: Poisson distribution over the non-negative integers.
members.extended:
Models the number of events occurring in a fixed interval when events
happen independently at a constant mean rate $\lambda$.  The
Poisson distribution is the limiting case of a Binomial as
$n \to \infty$ and $p \to 0$ with $np = \lambda$
fixed.

parameters.name: rate
parameters.description:
Mean rate $\lambda > 0$.  Determines both the mean and the
variance of the distribution.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Poisson
>>> dist = Poisson(rate=3.5)
>>> samples = dist.sample((100,))
>>> samples.shape
(100,)
>>> # log-probability at k=4
>>> dist.log_prob(lucid.tensor(4.0))
notes:
**PMF** (probability mass function):



$$
P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!},
\quad k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}
$$

**Moments**:

- Mean: $E[X] = \lambda$
- Variance: $\operatorname{Var}[X] = \lambda$
- Mode: $\lfloor \lambda \rfloor$

The log-probability is computed as
$k \log \lambda - \lambda - \log\Gamma(k+1)$, which is
numerically stable via the `lgamma` function.

The Poisson has no closed-form entropy (it is an infinite sum over all
non-negative integers), so `entropy` raises `NotImplementedError`.

parameters.name: validate_args

that out-of-constraint constructor arguments raise
`ValueError` at construction time rather than silently
producing `NaN` values later.

methods.name: batch_shape
methods.summary:
Shape of the batch of independent (but not identically parameterised)
distributions.

returns.description: A tuple of integers.  `()` for a single scalar distribution.

methods.summary: Shape of a single observation drawn from the distribution.

returns.description:
`()` for univariate distributions.  Non-empty for
multivariate distributions such as
`lucid.distributions.Dirichlet`.

A tensor of shape `batch_shape`.  Raises
`NotImplementedError` if the distribution has no
closed-form mean (e.g. `lucid.distributions.Cauchy`).

methods.name: mode
methods.summary: Most likely value of the distribution (the argmax of the density).

returns.description:
A tensor of shape `batch_shape`.  Raises
`NotImplementedError` if not implemented by the
