methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the distribution.

returns.description:
A tensor of shape `batch_shape`.  The variance is the
second central moment, $\text{Var}[X] = \mathbb{E}[(X - \mu)^2]$.
Raises `NotImplementedError` if not provided by the
concrete subclass.

methods.name: stddev
methods.summary: Standard deviation of the distribution.
methods.extended:
Computed as $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}[X]}$.  Concrete
subclasses may override this for numerical efficiency; the default
implementation delegates to `variance`.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon differential (or discrete) entropy.
methods.extended:
Defined as

$$
H[X] = -\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p}[\log p(x)]
$$

for a continuous distribution and similarly for discrete ones.
Measured in nats (natural logarithm base).

returns.description:
A tensor of shape `batch_shape`.  Raises
`NotImplementedError` if not implemented by the
concrete subclass.

notes:
Entropy quantifies the average amount of "surprise" or
uncertainty in a single draw.  Higher entropy means the
distribution is more spread out / less predictable.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw independent, identically distributed samples.
methods.extended:
The default implementation calls `rsample` and detaches the
result from the autograd graph, so gradients do not flow through
the returned tensor.  Discrete distributions that cannot be
reparameterised must override this method directly instead.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description:
Number of independent samples to draw.  The returned tensor
has shape `sample_shape + batch_shape + event_shape`.
Default is `()`, which returns a single sample with shape
`batch_shape + event_shape`.

returns.description:
A detached tensor (no gradient) of shape
`sample_shape + batch_shape + event_shape`.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Reparameterised sample — gradients flow through the noise.
methods.extended:
Unlike `sample`, `rsample` expresses the stochastic node as
a *deterministic* transformation of a parameter-free noise variable:

$$
X = g_{\theta}(\varepsilon), \quad \varepsilon \sim p_0(\varepsilon)
$$

This factorisation allows the gradient
$\nabla_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{X}[f(X)]$ to be estimated
cheaply via the pathwise (re-parameterisation) estimator, which
has much lower variance than the REINFORCE estimator.

Concrete distributions must override either this or `sample`.
Only distributions that admit a differentiable sampler set
`has_rsample = True`.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description:
Number of independent samples to draw.  The returned tensor
has shape `sample_shape + batch_shape + event_shape`.

returns.description:
A tensor attached to the autograd graph through the
distribution parameters.

raises.description:
If the distribution does not support reparameterised
sampling (`has_rsample = False`).  Use `sample`
instead in that case.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability (log-density) evaluated at `value`.
methods.extended:
For a continuous distribution with density $p(x)$ this
returns $\log p(x)$.  For a discrete distribution with
probability mass function $P(X = k)$ this returns
$\log P(X = k)$.

Working in log-space is numerically preferable to evaluating the
density directly: products of probabilities become sums of
log-probabilities, avoiding underflow for long sequences.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description:
Point(s) at which to evaluate the log-density.  Must be
broadcastable with `batch_shape + event_shape`.

returns.description:
Log-probability tensor of shape
`broadcast(value.shape, batch_shape + event_shape)[:-len(event_shape)]`.
In the scalar / univariate case this simplifies to
`broadcast(value.shape, batch_shape)`.

raises.description: If not implemented by the concrete subclass.

methods.name: cdf
methods.summary: Cumulative distribution function (CDF) evaluated at `value`.
methods.extended:
Returns the probability that a random variable $X$ drawn
from this distribution is less than or equal to `value`:

$$
F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} p(t)\, dt
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Point(s) at which to evaluate the CDF.

returns.description:
Values in $[0, 1]$ with the same shape as
`broadcast(value, batch_shape)`.

raises.description: If not implemented by the concrete subclass.

methods.name: icdf
methods.summary: Inverse CDF (quantile function / percent-point function).
methods.extended:
Given a probability $p \in [0, 1]$ returns the smallest
$x$ such that $F(x) \geq p$:

$$
Q(p) = F^{-1}(p) = \inf\{x \in \mathbb{R} : F(x) \geq p\}
$$

The quantile function is particularly useful for inverse-CDF
(Smirnov transform) sampling: if $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$
then $Q(U) \sim p$.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Probability values in $[0, 1]$.

returns.description:
Quantiles with the same shape as
`broadcast(value, batch_shape)`.

raises.description: If not implemented by the concrete subclass.

methods.name: prob
methods.summary: Probability density (or mass) at `value`.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Point(s) at which to evaluate the density/mass.

methods.name: __repr__
methods.summary: Concise string representation showing parameter shapes.

returns.description:
E.g. `"Normal(loc=(3,), scale=(3,))"` or
`"Bernoulli(probs=0.3)"` for a scalar.

members.summary: Abstract base for a probability distribution.
members.extended:
A distribution encodes a probability measure over a measurable space
and exposes a standard interface for sampling, evaluating
log-probabilities, and computing closed-form moments.  Every concrete
distribution in `lucid.distributions` inherits from this class.

Subclasses set:
  - `arg_constraints` — dict of param-name → `Constraint`,
    used by `validate_args` and to spell out the parameter domain.
  - `support` — `Constraint` for the random variable.
  - `has_rsample` — `True` for reparameterisable families.
  - `batch_shape`, `event_shape`.

Either `rsample` or `sample` (or both) must be overridden.

parameters.name: batch_shape
parameters.description:
Shape of independent, non-identical draws.  For a scalar
parameter this is `()`, for a vector parameter of length `n`
it is `(n,)`.  Default is `()`.

parameters.name: event_shape
parameters.description:
Shape of a *single* event (observation).  Univariate
distributions have `event_shape = ()`.  Multivariate
distributions such as `lucid.distributions.Dirichlet`
have a non-empty `event_shape`.  Default is `()`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description:
If `True`, parameter constraints and sample support are
validated at construction time and in `log_prob`.  Useful
during development; disable in production for speed.  `None`
inherits the class-level `_validate_args` flag.  Default is
`None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid.distributions as dist
>>> d = dist.Normal(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> d.batch_shape
()
>>> d.event_shape
()
>>> x = d.rsample((100,))  # shape (100,)
>>> x.shape
(100,)
notes:
**Shape semantics**

Every tensor returned by `sample` or `rsample` has shape



$$
\text{sample\_shape} + \text{batch\_shape} + \text{event\_shape}
$$

where `+` denotes tuple concatenation.  `log_prob` returns a
tensor of shape `sample_shape + batch_shape`, having reduced over
`event_shape`.

**Reparameterisation**

When `has_rsample = True` the sampler can be written as a
deterministic transformation of a fixed-distribution noise variable
$\varepsilon$:



$$
X = g_{\theta}(\varepsilon), \quad \varepsilon \sim p(\varepsilon)
$$

This allows gradients $\nabla_{\theta} \mathbb{E}[f(X)]$ to
be estimated with low variance via the pathwise derivative, which is
the backbone of the VAE objective (ELBO) and stochastic computation
graphs in general.

attributes.name: arg_constraints
attributes.description:
Maps each constructor parameter name to the
`lucid.distributions.constraints.Constraint` it must
satisfy.  Populated by each concrete subclass.

attributes.name: support
attributes.description:
Constraint describing the set on which the distribution has
positive density (or probability mass).  `None` means
unconstrained.

attributes.name: has_rsample
attributes.description:
`True` when the distribution implements
`rsample` — i.e., when the reparameterisation trick
(Kingma & Welling, 2013) is available and gradients flow through
sampled values.

attributes.name: has_enumerate_support
attributes.description:
`True` for finite discrete distributions that can enumerate
every possible outcome, enabling exact marginalisation.

members.name: ExponentialFamily
members.signature: ExponentialFamily(batch_shape: tuple[int, ...] = (), event_shape: tuple[int, ...] = (), validate_args: bool | None = None)
members.summary: Mix-in for exponential-family distributions.
members.extended:
An exponential-family distribution has a density of the form

$$
p(x \mid \eta) = h(x)\, \exp\!\bigl(\eta^\top T(x) - A(\eta)\bigr)
$$

where:

* $\eta$ are the *natural parameters* (also called canonical
  parameters),
* $T(x)$ is the vector of *sufficient statistics*,
* $A(\eta) = \log Z(\eta)$ is the *log-partition function*
  (log-normaliser), and
* $h(x)$ is the *base measure*.

This family encompasses most common distributions: Normal, Bernoulli,
Gamma, Beta, Poisson, Dirichlet, and many more.

Subclasses provide `_natural_params` and `_log_normalizer`
so that entropy can be derived from the standard exponential-family
identity

$$
H[X] = A(\eta) - \eta^\top \nabla_\eta A(\eta)
$$

Per-distribution overrides of `Distribution.entropy` remain
available for closed-form efficiency.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Bernoulli
>>> d = Bernoulli(probs=0.7)  # ExponentialFamily subclass
>>> isinstance(d, lucid.distributions.distribution.ExponentialFamily)
True
>>> d.entropy()
Tensor(...)
notes:
The exponential family structure implies:

* The log-normalizer $A(\eta)$ is convex in $\eta$.
* $\nabla_\eta A(\eta) = \mathbb{E}[T(X)]$ (the mean
  parameter).
* The Hessian $\nabla^2_\eta A(\eta)$ equals the covariance
  matrix of $T(X)$, hence is always positive semi-definite.

These properties underpin many elegant results in information geometry,
variational inference, and natural gradient methods.

**Members of this family in lucid.distributions** include
`lucid.distributions.Normal`,
`lucid.distributions.Bernoulli`,
`lucid.distributions.Categorical`,
`lucid.distributions.Gamma`,
`lucid.distributions.Beta`,
`lucid.distributions.Dirichlet`,
`lucid.distributions.Exponential`, and
`lucid.distributions.Poisson`.  Heavy-tailed families such
as `lucid.distributions.Cauchy` and Student's-t are *not*
members.

members.name: Cauchy
members.signature: Cauchy(loc: Tensor | float, scale: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(loc: Tensor | float, scale: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Cauchy distribution.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description: Location parameter (median and mode) $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Scale (half-width at half-maximum) parameter $\gamma > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Cauchy
>>> d = Cauchy(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> x = d.rsample((1000,))
notes:
The Cauchy distribution has PDF:



$$
p(x; x_0, \gamma) = \frac{1}{\pi \gamma}
\left[1 + \left(\frac{x - x_0}{\gamma}\right)^2\right]^{-1}
$$

The Cauchy is the Student's t-distribution with one degree of
freedom.  Because all moments of order $\geq 1$ are undefined,
it is a canonical example of a heavy-tailed distribution where the
sample mean does *not* converge to any value.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Reparameterised sample via the inverse-CDF method.
methods.extended:
Uses the quantile function of the Cauchy distribution:

$$
X = x_0 + \gamma \tan\!\bigl(\pi (U - \tfrac{1}{2})\bigr)
$$

where $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$.  Gradients flow
through both $x_0$ and $\gamma$.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  Default is `()`.

examples:
>>> d = Cauchy(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> x = d.rsample((500,))

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of `value` under the Cauchy distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x; x_0, \gamma) = -\log \pi - \log \gamma
- \log\!\left(1 + \left(\frac{x - x_0}{\gamma}\right)^2\right)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Real-valued observations.

examples:
>>> Cauchy(loc=0.0, scale=1.0).log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.0))
Tensor(-1.1447)

methods.name: cdf
methods.summary: Cumulative distribution function of the Cauchy distribution.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Real values at which to evaluate the CDF.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Cauchy distribution (in nats).
methods.extended:
$$
H(X) = \log(4 \pi \gamma)
$$
examples:
>>> Cauchy(loc=0.0, scale=1.0).entropy()  # log(4π) ≈ 2.531
Tensor(2.5310)

members.summary: Cauchy distribution on $\mathbb{R}$ — heavy-tailed, all moments undefined.
members.extended:
The canonical example of a *pathological* heavy-tailed distribution:
its tails decay so slowly that no integer moment (mean, variance,
skew, kurtosis) is defined.  The Cauchy is the Student's-t
distribution with one degree of freedom and arises naturally as the
ratio of two independent standard Normals.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description:
Location parameter $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ (median and mode).
The "mean" does *not* exist.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Scale parameter $\gamma > 0$ (half-width at half-maximum).

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Cauchy
>>> d = Cauchy(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> d.rsample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.0))
Tensor(-1.1447)
notes:
Probability density:



$$
p(x; x_0, \gamma) =
    \frac{1}{\pi \gamma}
    \left[1 + \left(\frac{x - x_0}{\gamma}\right)^2\right]^{-1}
$$

Cumulative distribution:



$$
F(x; x_0, \gamma) =
    \frac{1}{\pi} \arctan\!\left(\frac{x - x_0}{\gamma}\right) + \frac{1}{2}
$$

Properties (no moments — *all* are undefined or infinite):

* **Median / mode**: $x_0$
* **Mean / variance**: undefined (integrals diverge)
* **Entropy**: $H[X] = \log(4 \pi \gamma)$

**Sample mean does not converge** — the strong law of large numbers
fails for IID Cauchy variates.  Any finite-sample average of Cauchy
draws is itself Cauchy-distributed with the same parameters.

**Stability**: a sum of independent Cauchy variables remains Cauchy
(it is an $\alpha$-stable distribution with stability index 1).

Reparameterised sampling uses the inverse-CDF
$X = x_0 + \gamma \tan(\pi(U - \tfrac{1}{2}))$.

members.name: Exponential
members.signature: Exponential(rate: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(rate: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct an Exponential distribution.

parameters.name: rate
parameters.description:
Rate parameter $\lambda > 0$.  The mean of the
distribution is $1/\lambda$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Exponential
>>> d = Exponential(rate=2.0)
>>> d.mean  # 1/rate = 0.5
Tensor(0.5)
notes:
The Exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$ has PDF:



$$
p(x; \lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0
$$

It is the continuous analogue of the Geometric distribution and
describes the waiting time between events in a Poisson process.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Expected value of the Exponential distribution.
examples:
>>> Exponential(rate=4.0).mean
Tensor(0.25)

methods.name: mode
methods.summary: Mode of the Exponential distribution.
methods.extended:
The Exponential distribution is monotonically decreasing on
$[0, \infty)$, so the mode is always zero regardless of the
rate parameter.

$$
\text{mode} = 0
$$
examples:
>>> Exponential(rate=5.0).mode
Tensor(0.0)

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Exponential distribution.
examples:
>>> Exponential(rate=2.0).variance
Tensor(0.25)

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Reparameterised sample via the inverse-CDF method.
methods.extended:
Uses the transform $X = -\log(1 - U) / \lambda$ where
$U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$.  This is an exact
reparameterisation — gradients flow through $\lambda$.

$$
X = -\frac{\log(1 - U)}{\lambda}
$$

Subtracting from 1 (rather than directly using $U$) ensures
the argument to $\log$ is bounded away from zero.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  Default is `()`.

examples:
>>> d = Exponential(rate=1.0)
>>> x = d.rsample((500,))
>>> x.mean()  # approximately 1.0

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of `value` under the Exponential distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x; \lambda) = \log \lambda - \lambda x
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Non-negative real values $x \geq 0$.

examples:
>>> d = Exponential(rate=1.0)
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(1.0))  # -1.0
Tensor(-1.0)

methods.name: cdf
methods.summary: Cumulative distribution function of the Exponential distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
F(x; \lambda) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Non-negative real values at which to evaluate the CDF.

examples:
>>> Exponential(rate=1.0).cdf(lucid.tensor(1.0))  # 1 - e^-1 ≈ 0.632
Tensor(0.6321)

methods.name: icdf
methods.summary: Inverse CDF (quantile function) of the Exponential distribution.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Probability values $u \in [0, 1)$.

examples:
>>> Exponential(rate=1.0).icdf(lucid.tensor(0.5))  # log(2) ≈ 0.693
Tensor(0.6931)

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Exponential distribution (in nats).
methods.extended:
$$
H(X) = 1 - \log \lambda
$$

The entropy decreases as the rate increases (concentrated
distributions have lower uncertainty).
examples:
>>> Exponential(rate=1.0).entropy()  # 1 - log(1) = 1.0
Tensor(1.0)

members.summary: Exponential distribution on $[0, \infty)$.
members.extended:
Continuous distribution describing the waiting time between events of
a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$.  It is the continuous
analogue of the `lucid.distributions.Geometric` distribution
and the unique continuous distribution with the *memoryless* property.

parameters.name: rate
parameters.description:
Rate parameter $\lambda > 0$.  The mean of the distribution
is $1/\lambda$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Exponential
>>> d = Exponential(rate=2.0)
>>> d.mean  # 1/rate
Tensor(0.5)
>>> d.rsample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(1.0))
Tensor(-1.3069)
notes:
Probability density on $x \geq 0$:



$$
p(x; \lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}
$$

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}, \qquad
H[X] = 1 - \log \lambda
$$

**Memoryless property:**



$$
P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)
$$

Special cases / relations:

* $\mathrm{Exponential}(\lambda) = \mathrm{Gamma}(1, \lambda)$
* The sum of $k$ IID $\mathrm{Exponential}(\lambda)$
  variables is $\mathrm{Gamma}(k, \lambda)$ (Erlang).
* $-\log U / \lambda$ with $U \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$
  is the inverse-CDF sampler used by `rsample`.

members.name: Laplace
members.signature: Laplace(loc: Tensor | float, scale: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(loc: Tensor | float, scale: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Laplace distribution.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description:
Location parameter $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ (the mean and
median of the distribution).

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Scale (diversity) parameter $b > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Laplace
>>> d = Laplace(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> d.mean
Tensor(0.0)
notes:
The Laplace distribution has PDF:



$$
p(x; \mu, b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{|x - \mu|}{b}\right)
$$

It is also called the double-exponential distribution because it
resembles two mirrored Exponential densities placed back-to-back at
the location $\mu$.  It is frequently used in robust
statistics and as the prior inducing L1 (Lasso) regularisation.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Expected value of the Laplace distribution.
examples:
>>> Laplace(loc=3.0, scale=1.0).mean
Tensor(3.0)

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Laplace distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\operatorname{Var}[X] = 2b^2
$$
examples:
>>> Laplace(loc=0.0, scale=1.0).variance
Tensor(2.0)

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Reparameterised sample via the inverse-CDF method.
methods.extended:
Uses the closed-form quantile function:

$$
X = \mu - b \operatorname{sgn}(u) \log(1 - 2|u|)
$$

where $u = U - 0.5$ and $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$.
Gradients flow through both $\mu$ and $b$.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  Default is `()`.

examples:
>>> d = Laplace(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> x = d.rsample((200,))

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of `value` under the Laplace distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x; \mu, b) = -\frac{|x - \mu|}{b} - \log(2b)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Real-valued observations.

examples:
>>> Laplace(loc=0.0, scale=1.0).log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.0))
Tensor(-0.6931)

methods.name: cdf
methods.summary: Cumulative distribution function of the Laplace distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
F(x; \mu, b) = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2}
\operatorname{sgn}(x - \mu)
\left(1 - e^{-|x-\mu|/b}\right)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Real values at which to evaluate the CDF.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Laplace distribution (in nats).
examples:
>>> Laplace(loc=0.0, scale=1.0).entropy()  # 1 + log(2) ≈ 1.693
Tensor(1.6931)

members.summary: Laplace (double-exponential) distribution on $\mathbb{R}$.
members.extended:
Symmetric continuous distribution composed of two mirrored Exponential
densities placed back-to-back at the location $\mu$.  It is the
maximum-entropy distribution with a given mean and a given mean
absolute deviation, and is the prior whose negative log-density induces
the L1 (Lasso) penalty.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description: Location parameter $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ (mean = median = mode).

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Scale / diversity parameter $b > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Laplace
>>> d = Laplace(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> d.mean
Tensor(0.0)
>>> d.rsample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.0))
Tensor(-0.6931)
notes:
Probability density:



$$
p(x; \mu, b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{|x - \mu|}{b}\right)
$$

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \mu, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = 2 b^2, \qquad
H[X] = 1 + \log(2b)
$$

The Laplace has heavier tails than the Normal: its kurtosis is 6 vs. 3
for the Normal, and its tails decay exponentially rather than
quadratically in log-space.

Sampling uses the closed-form inverse CDF:



$$
X = \mu - b \operatorname{sgn}(U - \tfrac{1}{2})
    \log(1 - 2|U - \tfrac{1}{2}|),
\quad U \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)
$$

so `rsample` is exact and gradient-friendly through both
$\mu$ and $b$.

members.name: Beta
members.signature: Beta(concentration1: Tensor | float, concentration0: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(concentration1: Tensor | float, concentration0: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Beta distribution.

parameters.name: concentration1
parameters.description:
First shape parameter $\alpha > 0$ (also called
`concentration1`).  Controls how much mass is near 1.

parameters.name: concentration0
parameters.description:
Second shape parameter $\beta > 0$ (also called
`concentration0`).  Controls how much mass is near 0.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Beta
>>> d = Beta(concentration1=2.0, concentration0=5.0)
>>> d.mean  # α/(α+β) = 2/7 ≈ 0.286
Tensor(0.2857)
notes:
The Beta distribution has PDF:



$$
p(x; \alpha, \beta) = \frac{x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}}{B(\alpha, \beta)},
\quad x \in [0, 1]
$$

where $B(\alpha, \beta) = \Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)/\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)$.

Sampling uses the ratio-of-Gammas method:
$X = G_\alpha / (G_\alpha + G_\beta)$ where
$G_\alpha \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, 1)$.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Expected value of the Beta distribution.
examples:
>>> Beta(concentration1=1.0, concentration0=1.0).mean
Tensor(0.5)

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Beta distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\operatorname{Var}[X] =
\frac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2 (\alpha+\beta+1)}
$$
examples:
>>> Beta(concentration1=2.0, concentration0=2.0).variance
Tensor(0.05)

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the Beta distribution.
methods.extended:
Uses the ratio-of-independent-Gamma-samples identity:

$$
X = \frac{G_\alpha}{G_\alpha + G_\beta},
\quad G_\alpha \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, 1),\;
G_\beta \sim \text{Gamma}(\beta, 1)
$$

The result is **detached** since the underlying Gamma sampler is
rejection-based and does not support reparameterisation.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  Default is `()`.

examples:
>>> d = Beta(concentration1=2.0, concentration0=5.0)
>>> x = d.sample((500,))
>>> x.mean()  # approximately 2/7 ≈ 0.286

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of `value` under the Beta distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x; \alpha, \beta) =
(\alpha - 1) \log x + (\beta - 1) \log(1-x)
- \log B(\alpha, \beta)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Values in $(0, 1)$.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Beta distribution (in nats).
methods.extended:
$$
H(X) = \log B(\alpha,\beta)
- (\alpha-1)\psi(\alpha)
- (\beta-1)\psi(\beta)
+ (\alpha+\beta-2)\psi(\alpha+\beta)
$$

where $\psi$ is the digamma function and
$B(\alpha,\beta) = \Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)/\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)$.

members.summary: Beta distribution on the open interval $(0, 1)$.
members.extended:
Two-parameter continuous distribution that is the **conjugate prior**
of the `lucid.distributions.Bernoulli` / Binomial likelihoods.
When $\alpha = \beta = 1$ it reduces to a Uniform on
$(0, 1)$; for large $\alpha + \beta$ it concentrates
around $\alpha/(\alpha + \beta)$.

parameters.name: concentration1
parameters.description: First shape parameter $\alpha > 0$ (sometimes called *a*).

parameters.name: concentration0
parameters.description: Second shape parameter $\beta > 0$ (sometimes called *b*).

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Beta
>>> d = Beta(concentration1=2.0, concentration0=5.0)
>>> d.mean  # 2/(2+5) ≈ 0.2857
Tensor(0.2857)
>>> d.sample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.3))
Tensor(...)
notes:
Probability density on $x \in (0, 1)$:



$$
p(x; \alpha, \beta) =
    \frac{x^{\alpha - 1} (1 - x)^{\beta - 1}}{B(\alpha, \beta)}
$$

where $B(\alpha, \beta) = \Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)/\Gamma(\alpha + \beta)$.

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = \frac{\alpha \beta}
                       {(\alpha + \beta)^2 (\alpha + \beta + 1)}
$$

Mode: $(\alpha - 1) / (\alpha + \beta - 2)$ for
$\alpha, \beta > 1$.

Special cases / shapes:

* $\alpha = \beta = 1$ → $\mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$.
* $\alpha = \beta$ → symmetric around $0.5$.
* $\alpha, \beta \to \infty$ with fixed mean → concentrates as
  a Gaussian about the mean.
* Bimodal (U-shaped) when both $\alpha, \beta < 1$.

**Conjugacy**: observing $k$ successes out of $n$
Bernoulli trials updates `Beta(α, β) → Beta(α + k, β + n - k)`.

Sampling uses the ratio-of-Gammas method:
$X = G_\alpha / (G_\alpha + G_\beta)$ with independent
$G_\alpha \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, 1)$,
$G_\beta \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\beta, 1)$.  Since the underlying
Gamma sampler is rejection-based, samples are detached.

members.name: Chi2
members.signature: Chi2(df: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(df: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Chi-squared distribution.

parameters.name: df
parameters.description: Degrees of freedom $k > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Chi2
>>> d = Chi2(df=4.0)
>>> d.mean  # k = 4
Tensor(4.0)
notes:
$\chi^2(k)$ is a special case of the Gamma distribution:



$$
\chi^2(k) = \text{Gamma}\!\left(\frac{k}{2},\; \frac{1}{2}\right)
$$

It arises as the distribution of the sum of squares of $k$
independent standard Normal variables, and is fundamental in
hypothesis testing (e.g., goodness-of-fit tests, likelihood-ratio
tests).

members.summary: Chi-squared distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom.
members.extended:
Distribution of the sum of squares of $k$ independent standard
Normal random variables.  Foundational in classical statistics:
arises in the likelihood-ratio test (Wilks' theorem), Pearson's
goodness-of-fit, confidence intervals for variance, and many F-test
/t-test derivations.

Equivalent to a Gamma with shape $k/2$ and rate $1/2$:
$\chi^2(k) = \mathrm{Gamma}(k/2,\, 1/2)$.

parameters.name: df
parameters.description: Degrees of freedom $k > 0$.  Need not be an integer.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Chi2
>>> d = Chi2(df=4.0)
>>> d.mean
Tensor(4.0)
>>> d.sample((4,))
Tensor([...])
notes:
Probability density on $x > 0$:



$$
p(x; k) = \frac{1}{2^{k/2} \Gamma(k/2)}
          x^{k/2 - 1} e^{-x/2}
$$

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = k, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = 2k, \qquad
\mathrm{Mode} = \max(k - 2, 0)
$$

Special cases:

* $k = 1$ → squared standard Normal.
* $k = 2$ → $\mathrm{Exponential}(1/2)$.
* Sum: $\chi^2(k_1) + \chi^2(k_2) \sim \chi^2(k_1 + k_2)$.

By the central limit theorem,
$(\chi^2(k) - k)/\sqrt{2k} \to \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ as
$k \to \infty$.

members.name: Dirichlet
members.signature: Dirichlet(concentration: Tensor, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Construct a Dirichlet distribution.

parameters.name: concentration
parameters.description:
Concentration vector $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ with all
entries $> 0$.  The last dimension is the event (simplex)
dimension $K$; all preceding dimensions form the batch
shape.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Dirichlet
>>> d = Dirichlet(lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0]))
>>> d.mean  # proportional to concentration
Tensor([0.1667, 0.3333, 0.5000])
notes:
The Dirichlet distribution with concentration $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$
has PDF over the $K$-simplex:



$$
p(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\alpha}) =
\frac{1}{B(\boldsymbol{\alpha})}
\prod_{i=1}^{K} x_i^{\alpha_i - 1}
$$

where
$B(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \prod_i \Gamma(\alpha_i) / \Gamma(\sum_i \alpha_i)$.

Sampling uses the normalised-Gamma trick: draw independent
$G_i \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha_i, 1)$ then return
$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{G} / \sum_i G_i$.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Expected value of the Dirichlet distribution.
methods.extended:
Each component of the mean equals the normalised concentration:

$$
E[X_i] = \frac{\alpha_i}{\sum_j \alpha_j}
$$

returns.description: Mean vector on the simplex, shape `batch_shape + event_shape`.

examples:
>>> Dirichlet(lucid.tensor([2.0, 2.0])).mean
Tensor([0.5, 0.5])

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Dirichlet distribution (component-wise).
methods.extended:
$$
\operatorname{Var}[X_i] =
\frac{\mu_i (1 - \mu_i)}{\alpha_0 + 1},
\quad \alpha_0 = \sum_j \alpha_j,\;
\mu_i = \alpha_i / \alpha_0
$$

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the Dirichlet distribution.
methods.extended:
Uses the normalised-Gamma method:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \frac{\mathbf{g}}{\sum_i g_i},
\quad g_i \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha_i, 1)
$$

The result lies on the probability simplex and is **detached**.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  Default is `()`.

returns.description:
Simplex-valued samples of shape
`sample_shape + batch_shape + event_shape`.

examples:
>>> d = Dirichlet(lucid.tensor([1.0, 1.0, 1.0]))
>>> x = d.sample((100,))
>>> x.sum(dim=-1)  # all ones

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of `value` under the Dirichlet distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\alpha}) =
\sum_i (\alpha_i - 1) \log x_i - \log B(\boldsymbol{\alpha})
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Simplex-valued observations, last dimension is $K$.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Dirichlet distribution (in nats).
methods.extended:
$$
H = \log B(\boldsymbol{\alpha})
+ (\alpha_0 - K) \psi(\alpha_0)
- \sum_i (\alpha_i - 1) \psi(\alpha_i)
$$

where $\alpha_0 = \sum_i \alpha_i$, $K$ is the
number of categories, and $\psi$ is the digamma function.

members.summary: Dirichlet distribution on the $K$-simplex.
members.extended:
Multivariate generalisation of the `lucid.distributions.Beta`
distribution: a distribution over probability vectors
$\mathbf{x} \in \Delta^{K-1}$ with $x_i \geq 0$ and
$\sum_i x_i = 1$.  It is the **conjugate prior** of the
`lucid.distributions.Categorical` /
`lucid.distributions.Multinomial` likelihoods and a foundational
building block in topic models (LDA), Bayesian mixture models, and
population genetics.

The last dimension of `concentration` is the simplex/event
dimension; all preceding dimensions form the batch shape.

parameters.name: concentration
parameters.description:
Concentration vector $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ with all
entries $\alpha_i > 0$.  Shape `(..., K)`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Dirichlet
>>> d = Dirichlet(lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0]))
>>> d.mean  # α / Σ α
Tensor([0.1667, 0.3333, 0.5000])
>>> d.sample((4,))
Tensor([...])
notes:
Probability density on the $K$-simplex
($x_i > 0,\;\sum_i x_i = 1$):



$$
p(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\alpha}) =
    \frac{1}{B(\boldsymbol{\alpha})}
    \prod_{i=1}^{K} x_i^{\alpha_i - 1},
\qquad
B(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) =
    \frac{\prod_i \Gamma(\alpha_i)}{\Gamma(\alpha_0)},
\quad \alpha_0 = \sum_i \alpha_i
$$

Moments (with $\alpha_0 = \sum_i \alpha_i$,
$\mu_i = \alpha_i/\alpha_0$):



$$
\mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu_i, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X_i] = \frac{\mu_i (1 - \mu_i)}{\alpha_0 + 1},
\qquad
\mathrm{Cov}[X_i, X_j] = -\frac{\mu_i \mu_j}{\alpha_0 + 1}
\;\;(i \neq j)
$$

Special cases:

* $K = 2$ → $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_1, \alpha_2)$
  (after dropping one redundant coordinate).
* $\boldsymbol{\alpha} = \mathbf{1}$ → uniform over the
  simplex.
* $\alpha_0 \to \infty$ with fixed
  $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ → mass concentrates at
  $\boldsymbol{\mu}$.

**Conjugacy**: observing categorical counts
$\mathbf{n} = (n_1, \ldots, n_K)$ updates
$\mathrm{Dirichlet}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) \to \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \mathbf{n})$.

Sampling uses the normalised-Gamma method: draw independent
$G_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_i, 1)$ and set
$X_i = G_i / \sum_j G_j$.  Samples are detached.

members.name: Gamma
members.signature: Gamma(concentration: Tensor | float, rate: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(concentration: Tensor | float, rate: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Gamma distribution.

parameters.name: concentration
parameters.description:
Shape (concentration) parameter $\alpha > 0$.  Also known
as the shape parameter $k$.

parameters.name: rate
parameters.description:
Rate (inverse-scale) parameter $\beta > 0$.  The scale
is $\theta = 1/\beta$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Gamma
>>> d = Gamma(concentration=2.0, rate=1.0)
>>> d.mean  # α/β = 2.0
Tensor(2.0)
notes:
The Gamma distribution has PDF:



$$
p(x; \alpha, \beta) = \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}
x^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta x}, \quad x > 0
$$

Special cases include:

- $\text{Gamma}(1, \beta) = \text{Exponential}(\beta)$
- $\text{Gamma}(k/2, 1/2) = \chi^2(k)$

Sampling uses the Marsaglia–Tsang acceptance-rejection algorithm
(see module docstring); the accept rate is > 95 % so eight retry
rounds are more than sufficient.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Expected value of the Gamma distribution.
examples:
>>> Gamma(concentration=3.0, rate=2.0).mean
Tensor(1.5)

methods.name: mode
methods.summary: Mode of the Gamma distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\text{mode} = \frac{\max(\alpha - 1,\; 0)}{\beta}
$$

The mode is zero for $\alpha \leq 1$ (the density is
monotonically decreasing from infinity) and positive for
$\alpha > 1$.
examples:
>>> Gamma(concentration=3.0, rate=1.0).mode
Tensor(2.0)

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Gamma distribution.
examples:
>>> Gamma(concentration=4.0, rate=2.0).variance
Tensor(1.0)

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the Gamma distribution.
methods.extended:
Delegates to `_sample_standard_gamma` (Marsaglia–Tsang algorithm)
and scales by $1/\beta$ to obtain $\text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)$
samples.  The result is **detached** since the rejection-based
sampler does not support reparameterisation.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  Default is `()`.

examples:
>>> d = Gamma(concentration=2.0, rate=1.0)
>>> x = d.sample((500,))
>>> x.mean()  # approximately 2.0

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of `value` under the Gamma distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x; \alpha, \beta) = \alpha \log \beta
+ (\alpha - 1) \log x - \beta x - \log \Gamma(\alpha)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Positive real values $x > 0$.

examples:
>>> Gamma(concentration=1.0, rate=1.0).log_prob(lucid.tensor(1.0))
Tensor(-1.0)

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Gamma distribution (in nats).
methods.extended:
$$
H(X) = \alpha - \log \beta + \log \Gamma(\alpha)
+ (1 - \alpha) \psi(\alpha)
$$

where $\psi$ is the digamma function.
examples:
>>> Gamma(concentration=1.0, rate=1.0).entropy()  # Exp(1): H = 1
Tensor(1.0)

members.summary: Gamma distribution on $(0, \infty)$ — shape/rate parameterisation.
members.extended:
Two-parameter continuous distribution that generalises the
`lucid.distributions.Exponential` (shape $\alpha = 1$)
and `lucid.distributions.Chi2` (shape $k/2$, rate
$1/2$).  Widely used as the **conjugate prior** for the rate
parameter of a Poisson likelihood and for positive-valued
measurements such as waiting times, life-times, and rainfall amounts.

parameters.name: concentration
parameters.description:
Shape (concentration) parameter $\alpha > 0$.  Sometimes
called $k$.  When $\alpha$ is a positive integer the
Gamma equals the **Erlang** distribution (sum of $\alpha$
IID Exponentials).

parameters.name: rate
parameters.description:
Rate (inverse-scale) parameter $\beta > 0$.  Equivalent
scale parameterisation: $\theta = 1/\beta$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Gamma
>>> d = Gamma(concentration=2.0, rate=1.0)
>>> d.mean  # α/β
Tensor(2.0)
>>> d.sample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(1.5))
Tensor(...)
notes:
Probability density on $x > 0$:



$$
p(x; \alpha, \beta) =
    \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}
    x^{\alpha - 1} e^{-\beta x}
$$

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{\alpha}{\beta}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = \frac{\alpha}{\beta^2}, \qquad
\mathrm{Mode} = \frac{\max(\alpha - 1, 0)}{\beta}
$$

Entropy:



$$
H[X] = \alpha - \log \beta + \log \Gamma(\alpha)
+ (1 - \alpha)\psi(\alpha)
$$

where $\psi$ is the digamma function.

Special cases and relations:

* $\mathrm{Gamma}(1, \beta) = \mathrm{Exponential}(\beta)$
* $\mathrm{Gamma}(k/2, 1/2) = \chi^2(k)$
* Sum of independent Gammas with the same rate adds shapes:
  $\mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_1, \beta) + \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_2, \beta) = \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2, \beta)$.
* The ratio $G_\alpha/(G_\alpha + G_\beta)$ is
  $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$.

Sampling uses Marsaglia–Tsang acceptance-rejection — accept rate is
consistently above 95 %, so eight retry rounds are more than enough.
Because the kernel is rejection-based, `has_rsample = False` and
samples are detached.

members.name: Independent
members.signature: Independent(base_distribution: Distribution, reinterpreted_batch_ndims: int, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(base_distribution: Distribution, reinterpreted_batch_ndims: int, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise an Independent distribution wrapper.

parameters.name: base_distribution
parameters.description:
The underlying distribution.  Its `batch_shape` must have at
least `reinterpreted_batch_ndims` dimensions.

parameters.name: reinterpreted_batch_ndims
parameters.description:
Number of rightmost batch dimensions to move into `event_shape`.
Must satisfy
`0 <= reinterpreted_batch_ndims <= len(batch_shape)`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

raises.description:
If `reinterpreted_batch_ndims` exceeds the number of batch
dimensions of `base_distribution`.

methods.name: has_rsample
methods.summary: Whether reparameterised sampling is supported.
methods.extended:
Mirrors `base_dist.has_rsample`, so gradient flow is preserved
when the base distribution supports it.

returns.description:
`True` if and only if the base distribution supports
reparameterised sampling.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: Support of the distribution — delegates to `base_dist.support`.

returns.description: The support constraint of the underlying base distribution.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the distribution — delegates to `base_dist.mean`.

returns.description: Mean with the same shape as `base_dist.mean`.

methods.name: mode
methods.summary: Mode of the distribution — delegates to `base_dist.mode`.

returns.description: Mode with the same shape as `base_dist.mode`.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the distribution — delegates to `base_dist.variance`.

returns.description: Variance with the same shape as `base_dist.variance`.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Draw reparameterised samples — delegates to `base_dist.rsample`.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples — delegates to `base_dist.sample`.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log joint probability summed over re-interpreted event dimensions.
methods.extended:
Computes the base distribution's log-probabilities and then sums
over the rightmost `reinterpreted_batch_ndims` axes:

$$
\log p(x_1, \ldots, x_D) = \sum_{i=1}^{D} \log p_i(x_i)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Observations of shape `(*batch_shape, *event_shape)`.

returns.description: Log joint probability values of shape `batch_shape`.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Joint entropy summed over re-interpreted event dimensions.
methods.extended:
Because the components are independent:

$$
H[X_1, \ldots, X_D] = \sum_{i=1}^{D} H[X_i]
$$

members.summary: Re-interpret rightmost batch dimensions of a base distribution as event dims.
members.extended:
`Independent(base_distribution, reinterpreted_batch_ndims=n)` takes
the last $n$ dimensions of `base_distribution.batch_shape` and
moves them into `event_shape`.  Log-probabilities are *summed* over the
re-interpreted axes, turning a product of independent marginals into the
joint log-probability.

This is a pure structural wrapper — it does **not** change the sampler or
the underlying computation.  Its main use is to express

- **Diagonal multivariate Normals** from batches of scalar Normals, or
- **Independent Bernoulli likelihoods** over image pixels from a
  per-pixel Bernoulli batch.

parameters.name: base_distribution
parameters.description:
The underlying distribution.  Its `batch_shape` must have at least
`reinterpreted_batch_ndims` dimensions.

parameters.name: reinterpreted_batch_ndims
parameters.description:
Number of rightmost batch dimensions to absorb into `event_shape`.
Must satisfy `0 <= reinterpreted_batch_ndims <= len(batch_shape)`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Independent
>>> from lucid.distributions import Normal
>>> # Diagonal Normal: batch of D=4 scalars → single 4-d event
>>> base = Normal(lucid.zeros(4), lucid.ones(4))
>>> dist = Independent(base, reinterpreted_batch_ndims=1)
>>> dist.batch_shape, dist.event_shape
((), (4,))
>>> x = dist.rsample()
>>> x.shape  # (4,)
(4,)
>>> dist.log_prob(x)  # scalar — sum of 4 log-probs
notes:
Given `base` with `batch_shape = (B, D)` and `event_shape = ()`,
wrapping with `reinterpreted_batch_ndims=1` yields:

- `batch_shape = (B,)`
- `event_shape = (D,)`

The resulting `log_prob(x)` sums the $D$ scalar log-probabilities:



$$
\log p(x_1, \ldots, x_D) = \sum_{i=1}^{D} \log p_i(x_i)
$$

which is correct because the components are independent by construction.

**Entropy** is similarly summed:



$$
H[X_1, \ldots, X_D] = \sum_{i=1}^{D} H[X_i]
$$

(equality holds because independence implies
$H[X_1, \ldots, X_D] = \sum_i H[X_i]$).

The `has_rsample` property mirrors `base_dist.has_rsample`, so
gradient flow is preserved when the base distribution supports it.

members.name: kl_divergence
members.signature: kl_divergence(p: Distribution, q: Distribution)
members.summary: Compute the Kullback–Leibler divergence $\mathrm{KL}(p \,\|\, q)$.
members.extended:
The KL divergence is the expected log-ratio of two probability
measures $p$ and $q$ defined on the same sample space:

$$
\mathrm{KL}(p \,\|\, q) =
    \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p}\!\left[\log \frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\right]
= \int p(x) \log \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \, dx
$$

It is non-negative
($\mathrm{KL}(p \,\|\, q) \geq 0$, with equality iff
$p = q$ almost everywhere), is not symmetric in general
($\mathrm{KL}(p \,\|\, q) \neq \mathrm{KL}(q \,\|\, p)$), and
does not satisfy the triangle inequality — so it is a *divergence*,
not a metric.

Dispatch walks the registry built by `register_kl`: first an
exact class match, then the MRO of `type(p)` × `type(q)` for the
most-derived registered ancestor pair.  Falls back to a single-sample
Monte Carlo estimate when $p.\mathrm{has\_rsample}$ is `True`
and no analytical formula is registered.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Left-hand distribution.

parameters.name: q
parameters.description:
Right-hand distribution (must share the support / event shape
of `p`).

returns.description:
Non-negative tensor of shape `batch_shape` giving the per-batch
KL divergence in nats.

raises.description:
When no closed-form pair is registered and $p$ does not
support reparameterised sampling, so MC fall-back is unavailable.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Normal
>>> from lucid.distributions.kl import kl_divergence
>>> p = Normal(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> q = Normal(loc=1.0, scale=2.0)
>>> kl_divergence(p, q)
Tensor(...)
notes:
KL divergence appears throughout machine learning:

* **Variational inference**: the ELBO equals
  $\log p(\mathbf{x}) - \mathrm{KL}(q(\mathbf{z})\,\|\, p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}))$.
* **VAE training**: the encoder regulariser is
  $\mathrm{KL}(q(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x})\,\|\, p(\mathbf{z}))$, available analytically for
  Normal-vs-Normal pairs.
* **Maximum likelihood**: minimising negative log-likelihood is
  equivalent to minimising $\mathrm{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_{\theta})$.
* **Mode-seeking vs mode-covering**:
  $\mathrm{KL}(p\,\|\,q)$ is mode-covering in $q$;
  $\mathrm{KL}(q\,\|\,p)$ is mode-seeking — important for
  variational approximations.

members.name: register_kl
members.signature: register_kl(p_cls: type, q_cls: type)
members.summary: Register a closed-form KL implementation for a distribution pair.
members.extended:
Decorator used to add an analytical
$\mathrm{KL}(p \,\|\, q)$ formula to the global dispatch
registry, keyed on the *exact* (or ancestor) types of the two
arguments.  Once registered, `kl_divergence` will route calls
matching `(p_cls, q_cls)` (or any subclass thereof, via MRO walk)
to the decorated function.

parameters.name: p_cls
parameters.description:
Class of the "left" distribution $p$ (the reference
distribution in $\mathrm{KL}(p \,\|\, q)$).

parameters.name: q_cls
parameters.description: Class of the "right" distribution $q$.

returns.description:
A decorator that takes a two-argument callable
`fn(p, q) -> Tensor` and registers it in the internal KL
registry, returning `fn` unchanged so the decorated function
remains directly callable.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Normal
>>> from lucid.distributions.kl import register_kl, kl_divergence
>>> @register_kl(Normal, Normal)
... def _kl_normal_normal(p, q):
...     return (q.scale.log() - p.scale.log()
...             + (p.variance + (p.loc - q.loc) ** 2) / (2.0 * q.variance)
...             - 0.5)
>>> p = Normal(0.0, 1.0); q = Normal(0.0, 2.0)
>>> kl_divergence(p, q)
Tensor(...)
notes:
Dispatch precedence inside `kl_divergence`:

1. Exact class match `(type(p), type(q))`.
2. Most-derived ancestor pair found by walking the Cartesian product
   of `type(p).__mro__` and `type(q).__mro__` in order.
3. Monte Carlo fall-back (single `rsample` draw) when
   $p$ supports reparameterised sampling.
4. `NotImplementedError` otherwise.

Closed-form formulas are *strongly preferred* over Monte Carlo: they
are deterministic, zero-variance, and propagate gradients exactly.

methods.summary: Expected value via the law of total expectation: $E[X] = \sum_k \pi_k \mu_k$.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance via the law of total variance.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples from the mixture by ancestral sampling.
methods.extended:
The procedure is:

1. Draw component indices $k \sim \operatorname{Categorical}(\pi)$.
2. Draw one sample per component for the full output shape.
3. Gather the sample corresponding to the drawn component index.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability of the mixture evaluated at `value`.
methods.extended:
Uses the numerically stable log-sum-exp identity:

$$
\log p(x) = \operatorname{logsumexp}_k
\bigl[\log \pi_k + \log p_k(x)\bigr]
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Observation of shape `(*batch_shape, *event_shape)`.

members.summary: Finite mixture model where all components share the same distribution family.
members.extended:
`MixtureSameFamily` combines:

- a **mixture distribution** — a `Categorical` over $K$
  components that assigns mixing weight $\pi_k$ to component $k$,
- a **component distribution** — a single `Distribution` whose
  rightmost batch dimension has size $K$ (one set of parameters
  per component).

This encodes the generative process:

$$
k \sim \operatorname{Categorical}(\pi_1, \ldots, \pi_K), \quad
X \mid k \sim p_k(\cdot)
$$

**Sampling** is non-reparameterised because drawing the discrete index
$k$ creates a discontinuous path through the mixture weights.
For differentiable training through mixture models consider the
**ELBO** lower bound or use relaxed Categorical samples.

parameters.name: mixture_distribution
parameters.description:
A `Categorical` distribution over $K$ components.
Its `batch_shape` must be compatible with the leading batch
dimensions of `component_distribution`.

parameters.name: component_distribution
parameters.description:
Any distribution whose rightmost batch dimension equals $K$
(the number of components).  For example, a
`Normal(loc=..., scale=...)` with `loc.shape[-1] == K`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import MixtureSameFamily, Categorical, Normal
>>> # 2-component Gaussian mixture
>>> mix = Categorical(probs=lucid.tensor([0.3, 0.7]))
>>> comp = Normal(
...     loc=lucid.tensor([-2.0, 2.0]),
...     scale=lucid.tensor([0.5, 1.0]),
... )
>>> dist = MixtureSameFamily(mix, comp)
>>> samples = dist.sample((200,))
>>> samples.shape
(200,)
notes:
**Log-probability** is computed via the **log-sum-exp trick** to avoid
underflow when summing exponentially small terms:



$$
\log p(x) = \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k p_k(x)
           = \operatorname{logsumexp}_k
             \bigl[\log \pi_k + \log p_k(x)\bigr]
$$

**Mean** (law of total expectation):



$$
E[X] = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mu_k
$$

**Variance** (law of total variance):



$$
\operatorname{Var}[X] = \sum_k \pi_k \sigma_k^2
                        + \sum_k \pi_k (\mu_k - E[X])^2
$$

This decomposes into *within-component* variance (first term) and
*between-component* variance (second term).

internally converted to the lower-triangular Cholesky factor `L`
via `lucid.linalg.cholesky` or triangular inversion.

parameters.description: Mean vector $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^D$ of shape `(..., D)`.

parameters.name: covariance_matrix

parameters.description: Precision matrix $\Sigma^{-1}$ of shape `(..., D, D)`.

parameters.name: scale_tril

Lower-triangular Cholesky factor $L$ of shape `(..., D, D)`
with positive diagonal.

parameters.name: validate_args

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the MultivariateNormal: $E[X] = \mu$.

methods.name: mode
methods.summary: Mode of the MultivariateNormal: equal to the mean $\mu$.
methods.extended: The Gaussian is unimodal; its unique maximum is at the mean.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Marginal variances — diagonal entries of $\Sigma = LL^\top$.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Draw reparameterised samples via the Cholesky factorisation.
methods.extended:
$$
X = \mu + L \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_D)
$$

Gradients propagate through both $\mu$ and $L$.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the MultivariateNormal distribution.
methods.extended:
Computed via the Cholesky factor to avoid explicit matrix inversion:

$$
\log p(x) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\|L^{-1}(x-\mu)\|^2
            - \sum_i \log L_{ii}
            - \tfrac{D}{2}\log(2\pi)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Observation vectors of shape `(*batch_shape, D)`.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Entropy of the MultivariateNormal distribution.

members.summary: Multivariate Normal (Gaussian) distribution in $\mathbb{R}^D$.
members.extended:
`MultivariateNormal(loc=μ, ...)` defines the $D$-dimensional
Gaussian with mean vector $\mu$ and a covariance structure
expressed in one of three equivalent forms.  Internally all forms are
converted to the lower-triangular Cholesky factor $L$ of
$\Sigma$ (i.e. $\Sigma = L L^\top$), which is the numerically
preferred representation for both sampling and log-probability evaluation.

Specify **exactly one** of:

- `covariance_matrix` $\Sigma$ (positive-definite, $D \times D$),
- `precision_matrix` $\Sigma^{-1}$ (positive-definite, inverted via
  Cholesky internally), or
- `scale_tril` $L$ (lower-triangular with positive diagonal).

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description: Mean vector $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^D$ of shape `(..., D)`.

parameters.name: covariance_matrix
parameters.description: Full covariance matrix $\Sigma$ of shape `(..., D, D)`.

parameters.name: precision_matrix
parameters.description: Precision matrix $\Sigma^{-1}$ of shape `(..., D, D)`.

parameters.name: scale_tril
parameters.description:
Lower-triangular Cholesky factor $L$ of shape `(..., D, D)`
with positive diagonal entries.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import MultivariateNormal
>>> # 2-d isotropic Gaussian
>>> dist = MultivariateNormal(
...     loc=lucid.zeros(2),
...     covariance_matrix=lucid.eye(2),
... )
>>> samples = dist.rsample((50,))
>>> samples.shape  # (50, 2)
(50, 2)
>>> # Log-prob at the mean (maximum)
>>> dist.log_prob(lucid.zeros(2))
notes:
**PDF**:



$$
p(x; \mu, \Sigma) = (2\pi)^{-D/2} |\Sigma|^{-1/2}
\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)\right)
$$

**Log-PDF** (numerically stable via Cholesky, avoiding explicit inversion):



$$
\log p(x) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\|L^{-1}(x-\mu)\|^2
           - \sum_i \log L_{ii}
           - \tfrac{D}{2}\log(2\pi)
$$

where $\|L^{-1}(x-\mu)\|^2$ is the squared **Mahalanobis distance**
computed via triangular solve (no explicit matrix inversion).

**Moments**:

- Mean: $E[X] = \mu$
- Mode: $\mu$ (Gaussian is unimodal)
- Marginal variances: diagonal of $\Sigma = L L^\top$

**Entropy**:



$$
H[X] = \tfrac{D}{2}(1 + \log(2\pi)) + \sum_i \log L_{ii}
$$

**Reparameterised sampling** uses the Cholesky factorisation:



$$
X = \mu + L \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_D)
$$

Gradients propagate through $\mu$ and $L$ unobstructed.

attributes.name: scale_tril
attributes.description:
Cholesky factor $L$ (always populated, regardless of which
parameterisation was used for construction).

methods.summary: Construct a Log-Normal distribution.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description: Mean of the latent Normal $\ln X$.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Standard deviation of the latent Normal, $\sigma > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: Validate parameter constraints on construction.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the Log-Normal distribution.

methods.name: mode
methods.summary: Mode of the Log-Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\text{mode}(X) = e^{\mu - \sigma^2}
$$

The mode is strictly less than the mean, reflecting the
right-skewed nature of the distribution.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Log-Normal distribution.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Draw reparameterised samples.
methods.extended:
Samples are obtained by exponentiating Normal samples from the
underlying `Normal` base distribution:

$$
X = \exp(\mu + \sigma \varepsilon), \quad
\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)
$$

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Shape prefix for the batch of samples.

returns.description:
Strictly positive samples of shape
`sample_shape + batch_shape`.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the Log-Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
By the change-of-variables formula:

$$
\log p(x) = \log p_{\mathcal{N}}(\ln x) - \ln x
$$

where $p_{\mathcal{N}}$ is the density of the underlying
Normal distribution.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Strictly positive observation(s) $x > 0$.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon differential entropy of the Log-Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
H[X] = \mu + H[\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)]
     = \mu + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi) + \ln\sigma
$$

where the extra $\mu$ term accounts for the Jacobian of
the exponential transformation.

members.summary: Log-Normal distribution: $X = \exp(Y)$ where $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.
members.extended:
If a random variable $X$ has a Log-Normal distribution then its
natural logarithm $\ln X$ is Normally distributed.  The
probability density function is

$$
p(x \mid \mu, \sigma) =
    \frac{1}{x\,\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}
    \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right),
\quad x > 0
$$

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description: Mean $\mu$ of the underlying Normal $\ln X$.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Standard deviation $\sigma > 0$ of the underlying Normal.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: Enable constraint validation.  Default `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid.distributions as dist
>>> d = dist.LogNormal(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> x = d.rsample((100,))
>>> (x > 0).all()   # support is strictly positive
Tensor(True)
notes:
The Log-Normal arises naturally whenever a quantity is the product of
many independent positive factors (multiplicative growth), just as the
Normal arises from additive contributions.  Applications include
particle-size distributions, financial asset prices, and reaction
times.

**Mean and variance** of $X = e^Y$:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}, \qquad
\text{Var}[X] = (e^{\sigma^2} - 1)\,e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}
$$

Note that both grow super-exponentially in $\sigma$.

**Mode**:



$$
\text{mode}(X) = e^{\mu - \sigma^2}
$$

The mode is always less than the mean, reflecting right-skewness.

attributes.name: scale
attributes.description: Standard deviation of the latent Normal.

members.name: Normal
members.signature: Normal(loc: Tensor | float, scale: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.summary: Construct a Normal distribution.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description: Mean $\mu$.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Standard deviation $\sigma > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: Validate parameter constraints on construction.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Mean of the Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
For $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$,

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \mu
$$

methods.name: mode
methods.summary: Mode of the Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
The Normal density is symmetric and unimodal; its unique maximum
is at $x = \mu$.

$$
\text{mode}(X) = \mu
$$

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\text{Var}[X] = \sigma^2
$$

methods.name: stddev
methods.summary: Standard deviation of the Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\text{std}(X) = \sigma
$$

Overrides the base-class default (which would compute
`variance.sqrt()`) for a cheaper, exact result.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Draw reparameterised samples via the location-scale trick.
methods.extended:
Samples are generated as

$$
X = \mu + \sigma \varepsilon, \quad
\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)
$$

Because the stochasticity is isolated in $\varepsilon$
(which does not depend on the parameters), gradients flow back
through both `loc` ($\mu$) and `scale`
($\sigma$).

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Shape prefix for the batch of samples.

returns.description:
Samples of shape `sample_shape + batch_shape`, attached
to the autograd graph.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x \mid \mu, \sigma)
= -\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}
  - \log \sigma
  - \frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi)
$$

This is computed in a numerically stable form using
`log_scale` rather than squaring and dividing.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Observation(s) $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

methods.name: cdf
methods.summary: Cumulative distribution function of the Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
Expressed in terms of the Gauss error function:

$$
F(x) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}
       \!\left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Evaluation point(s) $x$.

methods.name: icdf
methods.summary: Inverse CDF (quantile function / probit function) of the Normal.
methods.extended:
$$
Q(p) = \mu + \sigma\,\sqrt{2}\,\operatorname{erfinv}(2p - 1)
$$

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Probability values $p \in (0, 1)$.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon differential entropy of the Normal distribution.
methods.extended:
The Normal maximises entropy among all distributions with fixed
mean and variance.  The closed-form entropy is

$$
H[X] = \frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi e\sigma^2)
     = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi) + \ln\sigma
$$

Measured in nats.

members.summary: Univariate Gaussian (Normal) distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.
members.extended:
The Normal distribution is arguably the most important distribution in
probability and statistics.  Its probability density function is the
iconic bell curve:

$$
p(x \mid \mu, \sigma) =
    \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
    \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right),
\quad x \in \mathbb{R}
$$

where $\mu$ is the *location* (mean) and $\sigma > 0$
is the *scale* (standard deviation).

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description:
Mean $\mu$ of the distribution.  May be any real value.
Can be a scalar, a 1-D tensor of means (producing a batch), or
any higher-dimensional tensor.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description:
Standard deviation $\sigma > 0$.  Must be strictly positive.
Broadcast-compatible with `loc`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: Enable parameter and support validation.  Default `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid.distributions as dist
>>> d = dist.Normal(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> d.mean
Tensor(0.)
>>> d.variance
Tensor(1.)
>>> x = d.rsample((5,))   # 5 standard-Normal draws
>>> lp = d.log_prob(x)    # evaluate log-density at those points
notes:
**Location-scale family**

The Normal distribution is closed under linear transformations: if
$X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ then
$aX + b \sim \mathcal{N}(a\mu + b, a^2\sigma^2)$.
In particular the *standard Normal* $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$
satisfies $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.

**Central Limit Theorem**

By the CLT, the (rescaled) sum of $n$ i.i.d. random variables
with finite mean and variance converges in distribution to a Normal.
This explains why the Normal appears naturally across virtually all
domains of science and engineering.

**Reparameterisation**

`has_rsample = True` — samples are drawn via the standard-Normal
reparameterisation trick:



$$
X = \mu + \sigma \varepsilon, \quad
\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)
$$

so gradients flow through $\mu$ and $\sigma$.

**Exponential family**

The Normal is an exponential-family distribution with natural
parameters $\eta_1 = \mu/\sigma^2$,
$\eta_2 = -1/(2\sigma^2)$ and sufficient statistics
$T(x) = (x,\, x^2)$.

members.summary: Concrete (Gumbel-sigmoid) relaxation of the Bernoulli distribution.
members.extended:
`RelaxedBernoulli(temperature=τ, probs=p)` defines a continuous
distribution over $(0, 1)$ whose samples are differentiable
surrogates for Bernoulli samples.  As $\tau \to 0$ the distribution
concentrates on $\{0, 1\}$, recovering the discrete Bernoulli.
As $\tau \to \infty$ the distribution approaches
$\operatorname{Uniform}(0, 1)$.

The **Concrete distribution** (Maddison et al. 2017) / **Gumbel-softmax**
(Jang et al. 2017) trick enables gradient-based optimisation through
discrete latent variables in variational autoencoders and related models.

parameters.name: temperature
probs
parameters.description:
Bernoulli success probability $p \in (0, 1)$.  Mutually
exclusive with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds $l = \log(p/(1-p)) \in \mathbb{R}$.  Mutually
exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import RelaxedBernoulli
>>> dist = RelaxedBernoulli(temperature=0.5, probs=0.7)
>>> samples = dist.rsample((100,))
>>> # Samples are in (0, 1)
>>> ((samples > 0) & (samples < 1)).all()
notes:
**Reparameterised sampling** (Gumbel-sigmoid trick):



$$
y = \sigma\!\left(\frac{l + g_1 - g_2}{\tau}\right),
\quad g_1, g_2 \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \operatorname{Gumbel}(0, 1)
$$

where $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the sigmoid function.  Gradients
propagate through both $l$ and $\tau$.

**Log-PDF** (Maddison et al. 2017, Eq. 2):



$$
\log p(y; l, \tau) = \log\tau + l
                     - (\tau+1)\log\!\left(e^{\tau\,\text{logit}(y) - l} + 1\right)
                     - \tau\,\text{logit}(y)
$$

where $\text{logit}(y) = \log(y/(1-y))$.
