Rows are automatically normalised to sum to 1.  Mutually exclusive
with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Unnormalised log-probabilities of shape `(..., K)`. Mutually
exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

raises.description: If both or neither of `probs` and `logits` are provided.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Draw a reparameterised sample via the Gumbel-softmax trick.
methods.extended:
Delegates to `lucid.nn.functional.gumbel_softmax` with
`hard=False`, ensuring the output lies strictly inside the open
$K$-simplex and gradients propagate through both `logits` and
`temperature`.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape of the output sample batch.

returns.description:
Samples on the open simplex of shape
`(*sample_shape, *batch_shape, K)`.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the Concrete distribution over the simplex.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description:
Point(s) $y$ on the open $K$-simplex, shape
`(..., K)`.

members.summary: Concrete distribution — a continuous relaxation of OneHotCategorical.
members.extended:
`RelaxedOneHotCategorical(temperature=τ, probs=p)` defines a
distribution over the open $K$-simplex whose samples are
differentiable surrogates for one-hot categorical samples.  As
$\tau \to 0$ the distribution concentrates on the $K$
vertices of the simplex (recovering one-hot samples); as
$\tau \to \infty$ samples approach the uniform distribution over
the simplex.

This is the **Gumbel-softmax** distribution of Jang et al. (2017) and the
**Concrete distribution** of Maddison et al. (2017).  The
straight-through estimator (`hard=True` in the sampler) rounds to
one-hot in the forward pass while using the soft sample for gradients.

parameters.name: temperature
parameters.description:
Temperature parameter $\tau > 0$.  Smaller values give
sharper (more discrete) samples.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Probability vector(s) of shape `(..., K)`.  Normalised internally.
Mutually exclusive with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Unnormalised log-probability vector(s) of shape `(..., K)`.
Mutually exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import RelaxedOneHotCategorical
>>> dist = RelaxedOneHotCategorical(
...     temperature=0.5,
...     probs=lucid.tensor([0.1, 0.4, 0.5]),
... )
>>> samples = dist.rsample((50,))
>>> samples.shape  # (50, 3) — lies on the open simplex
(50, 3)
>>> samples.sum(dim=-1)  # each row sums to ~1
notes:
**Reparameterised sampling** (Gumbel-softmax trick):



$$
y_k = \frac{\exp\!\bigl((l_k + g_k)/\tau\bigr)}
            {\sum_j \exp\!\bigl((l_j + g_j)/\tau\bigr)},
\quad g_k \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \operatorname{Gumbel}(0, 1)
$$

The result $y \in \Delta^{K-1}$ (open simplex) is differentiable
w.r.t. $l$ and $\tau$.

**Log-PDF** (Maddison et al. 2017, Eq. 1):



$$
\log p(y; l, \tau) = \log\Gamma(K)
                     + (K-1)\log\tau
                     + \sum_k (l_k - (\tau+1)\log y_k)
                     - K \operatorname{logsumexp}_k(l_k - \tau\log y_k)
$$

attributes.name: probs
attributes.description: Normalised probability vector (present when constructed with `probs`).

attributes.name: logits
attributes.description:
Unnormalised log-probability vector (present when constructed with
`logits`).

members.name: StudentT
members.signature: StudentT(df: Tensor | float, loc: Tensor | float = 0.0, scale: Tensor | float = 1.0, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(df: Tensor | float, loc: Tensor | float = 0.0, scale: Tensor | float = 1.0, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Student's t-distribution.

parameters.name: df
parameters.description:
Degrees of freedom $\nu > 0$.  Lower values produce
heavier tails: $\nu = 1$ is the Cauchy distribution,
while as $\nu \to \infty$ the distribution approaches
$\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description:
Location parameter $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$.  Default is
`0.0`.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Scale parameter $\sigma > 0$.  Default is `1.0`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import StudentT
>>> d = StudentT(df=5.0, loc=2.0, scale=0.5)
>>> d.mean
Tensor(2.0)
notes:
All three parameters are broadcast against each other so that
batches with mixed scalar / tensor parameters work naturally.
The resulting `batch_shape` is the broadcast shape of
`(df, loc, scale)`.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Expected value of the Student's t-distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
E[X] = \mu \quad (\nu > 1)
$$

The mean is only mathematically defined for $\nu > 1$.  For
$\nu \leq 1$ (e.g., the Cauchy distribution) the first
moment does not exist.  Following the convention of most
distribution libraries, this property returns $\mu$
unconditionally — callers are responsible for checking
$\nu > 1$ when that matters.
examples:
>>> StudentT(df=5.0, loc=3.0).mean
Tensor(3.0)

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Student's t-distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\operatorname{Var}[X] = \frac{\sigma^2 \nu}{\nu - 2}
\quad (\nu > 2)
$$

The variance is only finite for $\nu > 2$.  For
$1 < \nu \leq 2$ the distribution has a defined mean but
infinite variance.  For $\nu \leq 1$ neither moment
exists.  This property computes $\sigma^2 \nu / (\nu - 2)$
algebraically; the caller must guard against $\nu \leq 2$
as the result will be negative or infinite in those cases.
examples:
>>> StudentT(df=4.0, scale=1.0).variance  # 4/(4-2) = 2.0
Tensor(2.0)

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Reparameterised sample: gradient flows through the Normal variate.
methods.extended:
`T = loc + scale · z / sqrt(g / df)`  where  `z ~ N(0,1)`
is differentiable and `g ~ Chi²(df)` is detached (standard
practice — the marginal gradient w.r.t. df is not tracked).

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw non-differentiable samples from the Student's t-distribution.
methods.extended:
Wraps `rsample` inside a `no_grad` context so that the
returned samples have no gradient history.  Use `rsample`
directly when gradient flow through the location-scale
reparameterisation is required.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  Default is `()`.

examples:
>>> d = StudentT(df=3.0, loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> x = d.sample((200,))

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of `value` under the Student's t-distribution.
methods.extended:
$$
\log p(x; \nu, \mu, \sigma) =
\log\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{\nu+1}{2}\right)
- \log\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{\nu}{2}\right)
- \tfrac{1}{2}\log(\pi\nu)
- \log\sigma
- \tfrac{\nu+1}{2} \log\!\left(1 + \frac{z^2}{\nu}\right)
$$

where $z = (x - \mu) / \sigma$.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Real-valued observations.

examples:
>>> d = StudentT(df=1.0, loc=0.0, scale=1.0)  # Cauchy
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.0))  # -log(π) ≈ -1.145
Tensor(-1.1447)

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Student's t-distribution (in nats).
methods.extended:
$$
H = \log\!\left(\sigma\sqrt{\nu}\, B\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2},
\tfrac{\nu}{2}\right)\right)
+ \frac{\nu+1}{2}
\left[\psi\!\left(\tfrac{\nu+1}{2}\right)
 - \psi\!\left(\tfrac{\nu}{2}\right)\right]
$$

where $B(a,b) = \Gamma(a)\Gamma(b)/\Gamma(a+b)$ is the
Beta function and $\psi$ is the digamma function.

As $\nu \to \infty$ this converges to the entropy of
$\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, which is
$\tfrac{1}{2}\log(2\pi e\sigma^2)$.
examples:
>>> StudentT(df=1.0, loc=0.0, scale=1.0).entropy()  # Cauchy: log(4π) ≈ 2.531
Tensor(2.5310)

members.summary: Student's t-distribution with location, scale, and degrees of freedom.
members.extended:
`StudentT(df=ν, loc=μ, scale=σ)` defines the three-parameter
location-scale generalisation of Student's t.  It arises naturally in:

- **Bayesian inference**: the posterior predictive for a Normal likelihood
  with unknown mean and variance (Normal-InverseGamma conjugate model).
- **Robust regression**: as a heavy-tailed alternative to the Normal for
  outlier-tolerant models.
- **Limit behaviour**: as $\nu \to \infty$, the t-distribution
  converges to $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

parameters.name: df
parameters.description:
Degrees of freedom $
u > 0$.  Controls tail heaviness.
$
u = 1$ is the Cauchy distribution; $
u = \infty$
is the Normal.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description: Location parameter $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$.  Default is `0.0`.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Scale parameter $\sigma > 0$.  Default is `1.0`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import StudentT
>>> # Heavy-tailed (Cauchy)
>>> cauchy = StudentT(df=1.0, loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> # Near-Normal
>>> approx_normal = StudentT(df=100.0, loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> samples = approx_normal.rsample((500,))
notes:
**PDF**:



$$
p(x; \nu, \mu, \sigma) =
\frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+1}{2}\right)}
     {\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right)
      \sqrt{\pi \nu}\, \sigma}
\left(1 + \frac{(x-\mu)^2}{\nu \sigma^2}\right)^{-(\nu+1)/2}
$$

**Log-PDF**:



$$
\log p(x) = \log\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+1}{2}\right)
           - \log\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right)
           - \tfrac{1}{2}\log(\pi\nu)
           - \log\sigma
           - \frac{\nu+1}{2}\log\!\left(1 + \frac{z^2}{\nu}\right)
$$

where $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$.

**Moments**:

- Mean ($\nu > 1$): $E[X] = \mu$
- Variance ($\nu > 2$):
  $\operatorname{Var}[X] = \sigma^2 \nu / (\nu - 2)$
- The distribution has no finite variance for $\nu \leq 2$ and no
  finite mean for $\nu \leq 1$.

**Reparameterised sampling** uses the representation



$$
T = \mu + \sigma \cdot z \cdot \sqrt{\nu / g}
$$

where $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ is the **differentiable** variate
and $g \sim \chi^2(\nu)$ is **detached** (the Gamma sampler uses
rejection sampling whose path is not differentiable w.r.t. $\nu$).

members.name: AbsTransform
members.signature: AbsTransform()

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Zero everywhere — $|dy/dx| = 1$ outside the kink at zero.

members.summary: Element-wise absolute value $y = |x|$ (folded, **not** bijective).
members.extended:
Maps $\mathbb{R} \to [0, \infty)$ element-wise by folding the
sign.  Useful when composing with symmetric base distributions
(e.g. a Normal) to produce a half-Normal or folded-Normal
pushforward.  `bijective = False` and `sign = +1`.

Because both $x$ and $-x$ map to the same $y$,
the transform is non-invertible.  The pseudo-inverse used here is
the identity, under the convention that the preimage is assumed to
lie on the non-negative half-line.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import AbsTransform
>>> T = AbsTransform()
>>> T(lucid.tensor(-3.0))
Tensor(3.0)
notes:
Forward:  $y = |x|$.

Pseudo-inverse:  $x = y$ (assumes $x \geq 0$).

Log Jacobian determinant:



$$
\log\!\left|\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\right| = 0
$$

everywhere off the measure-zero set $\{x = 0\}$ where the map
is non-differentiable.

Folding the sign halves the density: composing with a Normal(0,1)
base gives the half-Normal density $p(y) = 2 \phi(y)$ for
$y \geq 0$, which requires special handling outside the
standard change-of-variable formula.  Lucid's
`TransformedDistribution` does not currently correct for
this folding factor — apply the $+\log 2$ constant manually
when needed.

members.name: AffineTransform
members.signature: AffineTransform(loc: Tensor | float, scale: Tensor | float)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Store the affine offset `loc` and multiplier `scale`.
methods.extended:
The caller is responsible for ensuring `scale > 0`; `sign` is
fixed at `+1` here so the transform is treated as increasing.

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Constant log-Jacobian $\log|\text{scale}|$.
methods.extended:
The Jacobian of $y = \text{scale} \cdot x + \text{loc}$
is just `scale`, so the log-determinant is constant in `x`.
We add `zeros_like(x)` so the result broadcasts to `x`'s
full shape (callers expect element-wise log-Jacobians).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Pre-transform input; used only for shape.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Post-transform output; ignored.

members.summary: Element-wise affine bijection $y = \mathrm{loc} + \mathrm{scale}\cdot x$.
members.extended:
The fundamental location-scale transform.  Composing with a
standard Normal yields $\mathcal{N}(\mathrm{loc}, \mathrm{scale}^2)$; it underpins virtually every reparameterised
sampler in the codebase.  Bijective with sign matching
`sign(scale)`; the implementation fixes `self.sign = +1`, so the
caller is responsible for ensuring `scale > 0`.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description: Additive offset $b$.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Multiplicative slope $a$ (assumed positive).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import AffineTransform
>>> T = AffineTransform(loc=1.0, scale=2.0)
>>> T(lucid.tensor(3.0))
Tensor(7.0)
notes:
Forward:  $y = b + a\,x$.

Inverse:  $x = (y - b) / a$.

Log Jacobian determinant (broadcast to `x.shape`):



$$
\log\!\left|\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\right|
= \log|a|
$$

For element-wise application (`event_dim = 0`) this is summed by
`IndependentTransform` if the caller reinterprets trailing
dims as event dims.

members.name: CatTransform
members.signature: CatTransform(transforms: list[Transform], dim: int = 0, lengths: list[int] | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(transforms: list[Transform], dim: int = 0, lengths: list[int] | None = None)
methods.summary: Store the per-partition transforms, concat axis, and partition lengths.

raises.description: If `transforms` is empty.

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Per-partition Jacobians concatenated back along `dim`.

members.summary: Apply different transforms to contiguous partitions along an axis.
members.extended:
Splits the input along `dim` into contiguous chunks of size
`lengths[i]` (or equal partitions if `lengths` is `None`),
applies the $i$-th transform to the $i$-th chunk, and
concatenates the results back along the same axis.  Differs from
`StackTransform` in that each partition can have a different
*length*, not just a single index.

parameters.name: transforms
parameters.description: One transform per partition.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Concatenation dimension.  Default `0`.

parameters.name: lengths
parameters.description:
Length of each partition.  If `None` the axis size must be
divisible by `len(transforms)` and equal partitions are used.

raises.description:
If `transforms` is empty, or if the axis size is not divisible
by `len(transforms)` when `lengths` is `None`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import ExpTransform, AffineTransform, CatTransform
>>> T = CatTransform([ExpTransform(), AffineTransform(0.0, 2.0)],
...                  dim=0, lengths=[2, 3])
>>> T(lucid.tensor([0.0, 0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0])).shape
(5,)
notes:
Forward (with $\mathbf{x}_i$ the $i$-th partition):



$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathrm{cat}\bigl([T_i(\mathbf{x}_i)]_{i=1}^{n},
                               \;\mathrm{dim}\bigr)
$$

Inverse: split, invert, re-concatenate.

Log Jacobian determinant: per-partition Jacobians concatenated back
along `dim`:



$$
\log|\det J| = \mathrm{cat}\bigl(
    [\log|\det J_{T_i}|(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i)]_{i=1}^{n},
    \;\mathrm{dim}\bigr)
$$

members.name: ComposeTransform
members.signature: ComposeTransform(parts: list[Transform])

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Store the ordered list of sub-transforms.

raises.description: If `parts` is empty.

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Cumulative log-Jacobian across the composition.
methods.extended:
Re-evaluates the chain of forward maps to obtain the
intermediate states needed by each sub-Jacobian, then sums the
contributions.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input to the first transform in the chain.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description:
Output of the last transform; ignored — the chain is
re-evaluated from `x`.

returns.description:
Element-wise sum of every sub-transform's log-Jacobian,
broadcastable to `x.shape`.

members.summary: Function composition of a list of transforms — left-to-right.
members.extended:
Implements the composite map
$T = T_n \circ T_{n-1} \circ \cdots \circ T_1$ so that the
first transform in `parts` is applied first.  The composite
Jacobian is the sum of the per-step Jacobians along the trajectory,
by the chain rule.  This is the workhorse for stacking normalising
flows.

parameters.name: parts
parameters.description:
Ordered list of sub-transforms.  Must be non-empty.  Their
`event_dim` values are reduced via `max`.

raises.description: If `parts` is empty.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import (
...     ExpTransform, AffineTransform, ComposeTransform)
>>> # Log-normal-like: y = exp(loc + scale * x)
>>> T = ComposeTransform([AffineTransform(loc=0.0, scale=1.0), ExpTransform()])
>>> T(lucid.tensor(0.0))
Tensor(1.0)
notes:
Forward:



$$
\mathbf{y} = T_n(T_{n-1}(\cdots T_1(\mathbf{x}) \cdots ))
$$

Inverse (transforms run in reverse with each one inverted):



$$
\mathbf{x} = T_1^{-1}(T_2^{-1}(\cdots T_n^{-1}(\mathbf{y}) \cdots ))
$$

Log Jacobian determinant (chain rule):



$$
\log|\det J_T(\mathbf{x})|
= \sum_{k=1}^{n}
    \log|\det J_{T_k}(\mathbf{x}^{(k-1)})|
$$

where $\mathbf{x}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$ and
$\mathbf{x}^{(k)} = T_k(\mathbf{x}^{(k-1)})$.  The
implementation re-evaluates the forward chain to collect the
intermediate states needed by each sub-Jacobian.

members.name: CorrCholeskyTransform
members.signature: CorrCholeskyTransform(dim: int)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Store the correlation-matrix dimension `dim` (must be `>= 2`).

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Log-Jacobian of the unconstrained-to-Cholesky correlation map.
methods.extended:
Combines the tanh contribution
$\sum_{i>j} \log(1 - \tanh^2(x_{ij}))$ with the
closed-form Lewandowski normalisation terms expressed directly
from the output diagonal entries of `y`.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Unconstrained parameters (strictly-lower-triangular real
entries).

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Output Cholesky correlation matrix.

members.summary: Bijection from $\mathbb{R}^{d(d-1)/2}$ to Cholesky factors of correlation matrices.
members.extended:
Maps an unconstrained vector of length $d(d-1)/2$ to the
lower-triangular Cholesky factor $L$ of a $d \times d$
correlation matrix (so $L L^\top$ is positive-definite with
**unit diagonal**).  This is the standard reparameterisation used by
Stan, by Lewandowski–Kurowicka–Joe (LKJ) priors, and by the reference
framework's correlation-matrix flow.  `event_dim = 2`.

The construction applies `tanh` to the free parameters, treats each
resulting value as a *partial correlation*, then normalises each row
of the lower triangle so the rows of $L$ have unit 2-norm
(which forces $L L^\top$ to have unit diagonal).

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Size $d \geq 2$ of the square correlation matrix.  The
unconstrained input has trailing dimension $d(d-1)/2$.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import CorrCholeskyTransform
>>> T = CorrCholeskyTransform(dim=3)
>>> x = lucid.zeros(3)  # d*(d-1)/2 = 3 free params
>>> L = T(x)  # 3x3 lower-triangular Cholesky of identity
notes:
Forward (row-by-row stick-breaking on the unit sphere):



$$
z_{ij} = \tanh(x_{ij}), \qquad
L_{ij} = z_{ij}\sqrt{1 - \sum_{k < j} L_{ik}^2}\;\;(i > j),
\qquad
L_{ii} = \sqrt{1 - \sum_{k < i} L_{ik}^2}
$$

Inverse:



$$
z_{ij} = \frac{L_{ij}}{\sqrt{1 - \sum_{k < j} L_{ik}^2}},
\qquad
x_{ij} = \mathrm{arctanh}(z_{ij})
$$

Log Jacobian determinant (closed form from Lewandowski et al., 2009):



$$
\log|\det J|
= \sum_{i>j} \log(1 - \tanh^2(x_{ij}))
+ \sum_{i=1}^{d-1} (d - i)\log L_{ii}
$$

Combine with the `lucid.distributions.relaxed.LKJCholesky`
distribution (or equivalent) to learn unconstrained correlation
matrices end-to-end.

members.name: CumulativeDistributionTransform
members.signature: CumulativeDistributionTransform(distribution: Distribution)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Store the distribution whose CDF/ICDF pair defines the transform.

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: $\log|F'(x)| = \log p(x)$ — the base distribution's log-density at `x`.

members.summary: Probability-integral transform $y = F(x)$ via a base distribution's CDF.
members.extended:
Pushes any continuous random variable through its own CDF to obtain
a $\mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$ random variable — the classical
*probability integral transform* of Smirnov.  Useful as a copula
building block, for goodness-of-fit testing, and for stitching
together heterogeneous flows.  `event_dim = 0`.

parameters.name: distribution
parameters.description:
Continuous distribution whose `Distribution.cdf` and
`Distribution.icdf` define the transform.  Both methods
must be implemented and the distribution must be continuous
(i.e., have a density) for the transform to be a true bijection.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Normal
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import CumulativeDistributionTransform
>>> T = CumulativeDistributionTransform(Normal(loc=0.0, scale=1.0))
>>> T(lucid.tensor(0.0))  # F(0) = 0.5
Tensor(0.5)
notes:
Forward (CDF):



$$
y = F(x)
$$

Inverse (quantile / ICDF):



$$
x = F^{-1}(y)
$$

Log Jacobian determinant equals the log-density of the base
distribution at $x$:



$$
\log\!\left|\frac{\partial F(x)}{\partial x}\right| = \log p(x)
$$

By the probability-integral transform, if $X \sim p$ then
$F(X) \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$.  Conversely, if
$U \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$ then $F^{-1}(U) \sim p$,
which is the inverse-CDF sampling identity used throughout
Lucid's `Distribution.rsample` implementations.

members.name: ExpTransform
members.signature: ExpTransform()

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: $\log|\partial y/\partial x| = x$ since $|dy/dx| = e^x$.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Pre-transform input.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description:
Post-transform output (`e^x`); ignored — the Jacobian is
cheaper to express via `x`.

members.summary: Element-wise exponential bijection $y = e^x$.
members.extended:
Maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0, \infty)$ element-wise; composed with a
Normal base distribution it yields a LogNormal.  Monotone increasing,
bijective, `event_dim = 0`.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import ExpTransform
>>> T = ExpTransform()
>>> x = lucid.tensor(0.0)
>>> y = T(x)
>>> T.log_abs_det_jacobian(x, y)
Tensor(0.0)
notes:
Forward:  $y = e^x$.

Inverse:  $x = \log y$ (defined for $y > 0$).

Log Jacobian determinant (element-wise):



$$
\log\!\left|\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\right| = x
$$

since $|dy/dx| = e^x$.

members.name: IndependentTransform
members.signature: IndependentTransform(transform: Transform, reinterpreted_batch_ndims: int)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Wrap `transform` and promote `reinterpreted_batch_ndims` batch dims to event dims.

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Sum the wrapped Jacobian over the reinterpreted trailing dims.

members.summary: Reinterpret `n` trailing batch dimensions of an inner transform as event dims.
members.extended:
A thin wrapper that does not modify the forward / inverse maps but
changes how the log-Jacobian-determinant is *aggregated*: the inner
transform's per-element $\log|\det J|$ is summed over the
reinterpreted trailing axes, producing a joint event Jacobian
instead of an element-wise one.  This is the bijection analogue of
`lucid.distributions.Independent` for distributions.

parameters.name: transform
parameters.description: Inner bijection applied to each element.

parameters.name: reinterpreted_batch_ndims
parameters.description:
Number of trailing batch dimensions to promote to event
dimensions.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import ExpTransform, IndependentTransform
>>> T = IndependentTransform(ExpTransform(), reinterpreted_batch_ndims=1)
>>> T(lucid.tensor([0.0, 1.0, 2.0]))
Tensor([1.0, 2.7183, 7.3891])
notes:
Forward / inverse: unchanged from the inner transform.

Log Jacobian determinant (with $n$ =
`reinterpreted_batch_ndims` minus the inner `event_dim`):



$$
\log|\det J|_{\text{outer}}
= \sum_{\text{last } n \text{ axes}}
  \log|\det J|_{\text{inner}}
$$

Use this whenever an element-wise transform (e.g. `ExpTransform`)
is applied to a vector that you want treated as a single event for the
purposes of computing $\log p(\mathbf{y})$ under the pushforward
distribution.

members.name: LowerCholeskyTransform
members.signature: LowerCholeskyTransform()

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Sum of $\log \sigma(x_{ii})$ over the diagonal.
methods.extended:
Off-diagonal entries contribute unit Jacobian; only the softplus
applied to the diagonal contributes the non-trivial factor
$\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)$, computed via the stable identity
$\log \sigma(x) = -\operatorname{softplus}(-x)$.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Pre-transform input matrix (last two dims square).

parameters.name: y
parameters.description:
Post-transform output; ignored — the log-Jacobian uses
`x`'s diagonal only.

returns.description: Per-sample log-Jacobian (reduces the last two matrix axes).

members.summary: Bijection mapping an unconstrained matrix to a positive-diagonal Cholesky factor.
members.extended:
The standard reparameterisation used to learn covariance / scale
matrices: a free $D \times D$ matrix is mapped to a lower
triangular matrix with strictly positive diagonal by zeroing the
strict upper triangle and applying `softplus` to the diagonal.
Composing with a base on $\mathbb{R}^{D \times D}$ produces a
pushforward over the cone of valid Cholesky factors.
`event_dim = 2`.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import LowerCholeskyTransform
>>> T = LowerCholeskyTransform()
>>> X = lucid.tensor([[0.0, 0.0], [0.5, 0.0]])
>>> L = T(X)  # softplus on diagonal, raw on strict lower
notes:
Forward (element-wise on a $D \times D$ input $X$):



$$
L_{ij} =
\begin{cases}
    \operatorname{softplus}(X_{ii}) & i = j \\
    X_{ij} & i > j \\
    0 & i < j
\end{cases}
$$

Inverse:



$$
X_{ii} = \operatorname{softplus}^{-1}(L_{ii})
       = \log(e^{L_{ii}} - 1),
\qquad
X_{ij} = L_{ij}\;\;(i > j)
$$

Log Jacobian determinant (summed over the matrix event dims):



$$
\log|\det J| = \sum_{i=1}^{D} \log \sigma(X_{ii})
             = -\sum_{i=1}^{D} \operatorname{softplus}(-X_{ii})
$$

Off-diagonal entries contribute unit Jacobian (identity map); only
the softplus applied to the diagonal carries a non-trivial factor.

For correlation-matrix factors (unit diagonal) use
`CorrCholeskyTransform` instead.

members.name: PowerTransform
members.signature: PowerTransform(exponent: Tensor | float)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Store the (element-wise) power `exponent`.

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: $\log|\partial y/\partial x| = \log|\text{exponent}| + (\text{exponent} - 1)\log x$.
methods.extended: Derivative of $y = x^{\text{exponent}}$.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Pre-transform input.  Must be strictly positive for the
`log x` term to be real.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Post-transform output (`x^exponent`); ignored.

members.summary: Element-wise power bijection $y = x^{\mathrm{exponent}}$.
members.extended:
Bijection on $(0, \infty) \to (0, \infty)$ (the input must be
positive; the implementation does not check this).  Useful for
Box–Cox-style reparameterisations and for mapping between Gamma
families with different shape parameters.

parameters.name: exponent
parameters.description:
Power $p$ applied element-wise.  Sign-aware: negative
exponents are admissible but flip orientation, and zero
exponents are not invertible (excluded).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import PowerTransform
>>> T = PowerTransform(exponent=2.0)
>>> T(lucid.tensor(3.0))  # 3² = 9
Tensor(9.0)
notes:
Forward:  $y = x^{p}$.

Inverse:  $x = y^{1/p}$.

Log Jacobian determinant:



$$
\log\!\left|\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\right|
= \log|p| + (p - 1)\log x
$$

Special cases:

* $p = 1$ → identity.
* $p = -1$ → reciprocal $y = 1/x$.
* $p = 2$ → squaring on the positive half-line.

members.name: ReshapeTransform
members.signature: ReshapeTransform(in_shape: tuple[int, ...], out_shape: tuple[int, ...])

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Validate that `in_shape` and `out_shape` have the same total size and store them.

raises.description: If `prod(in_shape) != prod(out_shape)`.

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Zero Jacobian — pure reshape is volume-preserving.

members.summary: Pure-shape reinterpretation of the event shape $y = \mathrm{reshape}(x)$.
members.extended:
Maps the trailing event tail of a tensor from `in_shape` to
`out_shape`.  This is a *volume-preserving* bijection — the
underlying storage is unchanged, only the interpretation of which
axes constitute the event differs.  The product of dimensions must
match.

parameters.name: in_shape
parameters.description: Event shape of the input tail.

parameters.name: out_shape
parameters.description:
Event shape of the output tail.  Must satisfy
$\prod_i \mathrm{in\_shape}_i = \prod_i \mathrm{out\_shape}_i$.

raises.description: If `prod(in_shape) != prod(out_shape)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import ReshapeTransform
>>> T = ReshapeTransform(in_shape=(2, 3), out_shape=(6,))
>>> T(lucid.zeros(2, 3)).shape
(6,)
notes:
Forward:  $\mathbf{y} = \mathrm{reshape}(\mathbf{x}, \mathrm{out\_shape})$.

Inverse:  $\mathbf{x} = \mathrm{reshape}(\mathbf{y}, \mathrm{in\_shape})$.

Log Jacobian determinant:



$$
\log|\det J| = 0
$$

everywhere — a reshape preserves Lebesgue measure exactly.  Useful
for stitching together transforms that expect different event
layouts (e.g. flatten a matrix event for a Cholesky-style
transform, then reshape back).

members.name: SigmoidTransform
members.signature: SigmoidTransform()

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Log-Jacobian $\log\bigl(y\,(1-y)\bigr)$ of the sigmoid map.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Pre-transform input (sigmoid logit); ignored — the
log-Jacobian is cheaper to express via `y`.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Post-transform output in $(0, 1)$.

members.summary: Element-wise sigmoid bijection $y = \sigma(x) = 1/(1 + e^{-x})$.
members.extended:
Maps $\mathbb{R} \to (0, 1)$ element-wise.  The natural
unconstrained → probability transform — composed with a base
distribution on $\mathbb{R}$, the pushforward lives on the
unit interval.  Monotone increasing, bijective, `event_dim = 0`.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import SigmoidTransform
>>> T = SigmoidTransform()
>>> y = T(lucid.tensor(0.0))  # σ(0) = 0.5
Tensor(0.5)
notes:
Forward:  $y = \sigma(x) = 1/(1 + e^{-x})$.

Inverse (logit):
$x = \log\!\bigl(y/(1-y)\bigr)$.

Log Jacobian determinant:



$$
\log\!\left|\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\right|
= \log\bigl(\sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))\bigr)
= \log y + \log(1 - y)
$$

The transform is closely related to the `StickBreakingTransform`
(which generalises to the simplex) and to `TanhTransform`
(which targets $(-1, 1)$).

members.name: SoftmaxTransform
members.signature: SoftmaxTransform()

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Pseudo-Jacobian $\sum_k \log y_k$ for the over-parameterised softmax.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description:
Pre-softmax logits.  Used only for shape; the formula
collapses through `y`.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Post-softmax probabilities in the simplex.

returns.description: $\sum_k \log y_k$, reduced over the last axis.

members.summary: Softmax transform mapping $\mathbb{R}^K \to \Delta^{K-1}$.
members.extended:
Pushes an unconstrained vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^K$
onto the open $K$-simplex via
$\mathbf{y} = \mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{x})$.  Operates on the
*last* axis; `event_dim = 1`.

The transform is over-parameterised: any constant shift along the
softmax axis ($\mathbf{x} \to \mathbf{x} + c\mathbf{1}$)
yields the same $\mathbf{y}$, so it is not a true bijection.
The standard convention used here is to anchor the inverse at
$\mathbf{x} = \log \mathbf{y}$ (un-normalised
log-probabilities), which is one canonical preimage.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import SoftmaxTransform
>>> T = SoftmaxTransform()
>>> T(lucid.tensor([0.0, 1.0, 2.0]))
Tensor([...])
notes:
Forward:



$$
y_k = \frac{e^{x_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{x_j}}
$$

Inverse (canonical anchor):



$$
x_k = \log y_k
$$

Pseudo-Jacobian used in change-of-variable bookkeeping:



$$
\log|\det J| = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log y_k
$$

This is the convention that keeps the simplex-valued pushforward
consistent in flow stacks; for a *true* bijection between
$\mathbb{R}^{K-1}$ and the simplex use
`StickBreakingTransform` instead.

members.name: StackTransform
members.signature: StackTransform(transforms: list[Transform], dim: int = 0)

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Store the per-slice transforms and the stacking axis `dim`.

raises.description: If `transforms` is empty.

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Per-slice Jacobians stacked back along `dim`.

members.summary: Apply a list of transforms to indexed slices along a stack axis.
members.extended:
The $k$-th transform is applied to the $k$-th slice
obtained by `Tensor.unbind` along `dim`, and the results
are stacked back along the same axis.  This is the heterogeneous
counterpart of `IndependentTransform`: different bijections
can be applied to different coordinates of an event vector.

parameters.name: transforms
parameters.description:
One transform per slice.  Must be non-empty and the same length
as `x.shape[dim]`.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Dimension along which to slice.  Default `0`.

raises.description:
If `transforms` is empty, or if the size of `dim` does not
match `len(transforms)` at call time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import ExpTransform, TanhTransform, StackTransform
>>> T = StackTransform([ExpTransform(), TanhTransform()], dim=-1)
>>> T(lucid.tensor([0.0, 0.0]))  # (exp(0), tanh(0)) = (1, 0)
Tensor([1.0, 0.0])
notes:
Forward (with $\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{x}[\ldots, k, \ldots]$):



$$
\mathbf{y}_k = T_k(\mathbf{x}_k)
$$

Inverse:



$$
\mathbf{x}_k = T_k^{-1}(\mathbf{y}_k)
$$

Log Jacobian determinant (per-slice values stacked back along `dim`):



$$
\log|\det J|_k = \log|\det J_{T_k}|(\mathbf{x}_k, \mathbf{y}_k)
$$

Useful for "block" flows where each coordinate of the latent vector
has its own bijection — e.g., applying `ExpTransform` to
positive components and `TanhTransform` to bounded ones.

members.name: StickBreakingTransform
members.signature: StickBreakingTransform()

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Standard simplex-to-$\mathbb{R}^{K-1}$ log-Jacobian.
methods.extended: $\log|\det J| = \sum_k \bigl[\log y_k + \log\!\bigl(1 - \sum_{j<k} y_j\bigr)\bigr]$.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Unconstrained $(K-1)$-vectors.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description:
Corresponding $K$-simplex points (output of the
forward map).

members.summary: Logistic stick-breaking bijection $\mathbb{R}^{K-1} \to \Delta^{K-1}$.
members.extended:
A **true** bijection between unconstrained $(K-1)$-vectors and
the $K$-simplex (i.e. one extra dimension is broken off as the
residual stick).  Unlike `SoftmaxTransform` it has the
correct dimensionality and a tractable log-Jacobian determinant,
making it the preferred choice for normalising flows over
probability vectors and for unconstrained reparameterisations of
`lucid.distributions.Dirichlet` priors.  `event_dim = 1`.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import StickBreakingTransform
>>> T = StickBreakingTransform()
>>> y = T(lucid.tensor([0.0, 0.0]))  # maps to a Dirichlet(1,1,1) sample
>>> y.sum()
Tensor(1.0)
notes:
Forward "stick breaking" (with $z_k = \sigma(x_k - \log(K-k))$):



$$
y_k = z_k \prod_{j < k}(1 - z_j),
\quad
y_{K-1} = \prod_{j=0}^{K-2}(1 - z_j)
$$

The last component is the residual stick remaining after the first
$K - 1$ breaks.  Each $y_k > 0$ and $\sum_k y_k = 1$
by construction.

Inverse (back-solve the stick lengths):



$$
z_k = \frac{y_k}{1 - \sum_{j < k} y_j},
\qquad
x_k = \mathrm{logit}(z_k) + \log(K - k)
$$

Log Jacobian determinant:



$$
\log|\det J| =
\sum_{k=0}^{K-2}
\bigl[\log y_k + \log(1 - \textstyle\sum_{j < k} y_j) + \log(1 - z_k)\bigr]
$$

where the $-\log(K-k)$ shifts ensure the uniform Dirichlet
corresponds to $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$.

members.name: TanhTransform
members.signature: TanhTransform()

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Numerically-stable $\log(1 - \tanh^2(x))$.
methods.extended:
Computed as $2\bigl(\log 2 - x - \operatorname{softplus}(-2x)\bigr)$
— the stable form used by the reference framework that avoids
the catastrophic `log(1 - 1)` cancellation when `|x|` is
large.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Pre-transform input.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description:
Post-transform output (`tanh(x)`); ignored — the
log-Jacobian is expressed in `x`.

members.summary: Element-wise hyperbolic tangent bijection $y = \tanh(x)$.
members.extended:
Maps $\mathbb{R} \to (-1, 1)$ element-wise.  Commonly used in
reinforcement learning to push a Normal policy through `tanh` so
actions live in a bounded box (SAC and friends).  Monotone
increasing, bijective, `event_dim = 0`.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import TanhTransform
>>> T = TanhTransform()
>>> T(lucid.tensor(0.0))  # tanh(0) = 0
Tensor(0.0)
notes:
Forward:  $y = \tanh(x)$.

Inverse:
$x = \tfrac{1}{2}\log\!\bigl((1+y)/(1-y)\bigr)$
(the inverse hyperbolic tangent).

Log Jacobian determinant (numerically stable form):



$$
\log\!\left|\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\right|
= \log\bigl(1 - \tanh^2(x)\bigr)
= 2 \bigl(\log 2 - x - \operatorname{softplus}(-2x)\bigr)
$$

This formulation avoids overflow / underflow when $|x|$ is
large, which the naive form $\log(1 - \tanh^2 x)$ would suffer
(since $\tanh(x) \to \pm 1$).

members.name: Transform
members.signature: Transform()

methods.name: __init__
methods.summary: Initialise the transform with an empty inverse cache.

methods.name: inv
methods.summary: Lazy inverse — caches an `_InverseTransform` view.

methods.name: __call__
methods.summary: Apply the forward map `T(x)` by delegating to `_call`.

methods.name: log_abs_det_jacobian
methods.summary: Log-absolute-determinant of the Jacobian $\partial T / \partial x$.
methods.extended:
Subclasses must override and return
$\log|\det \partial y / \partial x|$ broadcast over the
input batch dimensions.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Pre-transform input.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description:
Post-transform output (typically `y = self(x)`).  Some
transforms can compute the log-Jacobian in terms of
`y` more cheaply than `x`, hence both are required.

methods.name: __repr__
methods.summary: Return a developer-facing string representation of the instance.

members.summary: Abstract bijection between two measurable spaces.
members.extended:
A `Transform` represents an invertible map
$T : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ together with its
log-absolute-determinant Jacobian, used to push a base distribution
through a measurable bijection.  Composed with a base distribution
via `TransformedDistribution`, it implements normalising flows,
coupling layers, and standard change-of-variable arguments.

The change-of-variable formula for densities is

$$
\log p_Y(\mathbf{y}) = \log p_X(\mathbf{x})
- \log \left|\det \frac{\partial T(\mathbf{x})}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|,
\quad \mathbf{y} = T(\mathbf{x})
$$

so the per-transform Jacobian determinant must be tractable.

Subclasses must override:

* `_call` — forward map $\mathbf{y} = T(\mathbf{x})$.
* `_inverse` — inverse map
  $\mathbf{x} = T^{-1}(\mathbf{y})$.
* `log_abs_det_jacobian` —
  $\log \bigl|\det \partial T / \partial \mathbf{x}\bigr|$,
  broadcast over the batch dimensions of the input.
examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import ExpTransform, AffineTransform, ComposeTransform
>>> # y = exp(2*x + 1) — a log-normal-like transform
>>> T = ComposeTransform([AffineTransform(loc=1.0, scale=2.0), ExpTransform()])
>>> x = lucid.tensor(0.0)
>>> y = T(x)
>>> T.log_abs_det_jacobian(x, y)
Tensor(...)
notes:
Composition and inversion are provided structurally:

* `ComposeTransform` — $T = T_n \circ \cdots \circ T_1$.
* `~transform` / `inv` — lazy `_InverseTransform` view.

so users can build complex bijectors without manually tracking
Jacobian terms.

attributes.name: bijective
attributes.description:
`True` if the transform is invertible (most are; notable
exception: `AbsTransform`).

attributes.name: sign
attributes.description:
$+1$ for monotone-increasing scalar bijections,
$-1$ for monotone-decreasing.  Used by the change-of-variable
machinery to track CDF orientation.

attributes.name: event_dim
attributes.description:
Number of trailing tensor dimensions that the transform treats
as a single event.  `0` for element-wise maps,
$\geq 1$ for vector / matrix transforms (e.g.
`SoftmaxTransform` has `event_dim = 1`,
`LowerCholeskyTransform` has `event_dim = 2`).

members.name: TransformedDistribution
members.signature: TransformedDistribution(base_distribution: Distribution, transforms: Transform | list[Transform], validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(base_distribution: Distribution, transforms: Transform | list[Transform], validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a transformed distribution.

parameters.name: base_distribution
parameters.description:
Underlying distribution whose samples will be pushed through
`transforms`.

parameters.name: transforms
parameters.description: A single transform or an ordered list applied left-to-right.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: Forwarded to `Distribution`.

methods.name: has_rsample
methods.summary: Whether reparameterised sampling is available — inherited from the base distribution.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Push a reparameterised base sample through the transform chain.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Push a (non-reparameterised) base sample through the transform chain.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Evaluate the log-density of `value` under the transformed distribution.
methods.extended:
Uses the change-of-variable formula:

$$
\log p(y) = \log p_{\text{base}}(T^{-1}(y))
    - \sum_i \log\!\bigl|\det \partial T_i / \partial x_i\bigr|
$$

with the chain unwound by walking the transforms in reverse.

members.summary: Pushforward of a base distribution through a (composite) bijector.
members.extended:
Constructs a new `Distribution` whose samples are obtained by
pushing samples from `base_distribution` through the supplied
chain of `Transform` instances, with `log_prob`
accounting for the Jacobian correction via the change-of-variable
formula.  This is the canonical way to build normalising flows in
Lucid: stack any number of bijections on top of a tractable base
(typically a Normal) to obtain expressive densities while retaining
exact log-likelihood evaluation and reparameterised sampling.

parameters.name: base_distribution
parameters.description:
Underlying distribution whose samples will be pushed through
`transforms`.

parameters.name: transforms
parameters.description:
A single transform or an ordered list applied left-to-right.
Internally wrapped in a list.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: Forwarded to `Distribution`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Normal
>>> from lucid.distributions.transforms import (
...     ExpTransform, TransformedDistribution)
>>> # LogNormal = ExpTransform(Normal(0, 1))
>>> log_normal = TransformedDistribution(Normal(loc=0.0, scale=1.0), [ExpTransform()])
>>> log_normal.rsample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> log_normal.log_prob(lucid.tensor(1.0))
Tensor(...)
notes:
Sampling (with $T = T_n \circ \cdots \circ T_1$ the composite
bijector):



$$
Y = T(X), \quad X \sim p_{\text{base}}
$$

Reparameterised sampling is available iff the base distribution
supports it (`has_rsample` is forwarded).

Density (change of variables):



$$
\log p_Y(\mathbf{y}) =
    \log p_X(T^{-1}(\mathbf{y}))
    - \sum_{i=1}^{n}
        \log\!\left|\det
        \frac{\partial T_i(\mathbf{x}_{i-1})}{\partial \mathbf{x}_{i-1}}\right|
$$

where $\mathbf{x}_0 = T^{-1}(\mathbf{y})$ and
$\mathbf{x}_i = T_i(\mathbf{x}_{i-1})$.  The implementation
walks the transforms in reverse, inverting one step at a time and
accumulating the Jacobian correction.

methods.signature: __init__(probs: Tensor | float | None = None, logits: Tensor | float | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Continuous Bernoulli distribution.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Parameter $p \in [0, 1]$.  Mutually exclusive with
`logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds $l = \log(p/(1-p)) \in \mathbb{R}$.  Mutually
exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: `E[X²] − E[X]²`; `E[X²]` computed via the same normaliser trick.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Reparameterised sample via the closed-form icdf.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: `x · l − softplus(l) + log C(p)`.

members.summary: Continuous Bernoulli distribution on $[0, 1]$.
members.extended:
A continuous relaxation of the `lucid.distributions.Bernoulli`
distribution introduced by Loaiza-Ganem & Cunningham (2019) to fix the
well-known bias incurred by VAE decoders that use a Bernoulli
likelihood on real-valued image pixels in $[0, 1]$.  Unlike the
naive "Bernoulli on [0,1]" likelihood, the Continuous Bernoulli has a
proper normalising constant and yields unbiased ELBO gradients.

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Parameter $\lambda \in [0, 1]$.  Note that
$\mathbb{E}[X] \neq \lambda$ in general — see Notes for
the closed-form mean.  Mutually exclusive with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Log-odds $\ell = \log(\lambda / (1 - \lambda))$.  Mutually
exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import ContinuousBernoulli
>>> d = ContinuousBernoulli(probs=0.7)
>>> d.rsample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.5))
Tensor(...)
notes:
Probability density on $x \in [0, 1]$:



$$
p(x; \lambda) = C(\lambda)\, \lambda^x (1 - \lambda)^{1 - x}
$$

where the normalising constant is



$$
C(\lambda) =
\begin{cases}
    \dfrac{2 \tanh^{-1}(1 - 2\lambda)}{1 - 2\lambda}
        & \lambda \neq \tfrac{1}{2} \\[4pt]
    2 & \lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}
\end{cases}
$$

Mean (closed form):



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] =
\begin{cases}
    \dfrac{\lambda}{2\lambda - 1} +
    \dfrac{1}{2 \tanh^{-1}(1 - 2\lambda)}
        & \lambda \neq \tfrac{1}{2} \\[4pt]
    \tfrac{1}{2} & \lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}
\end{cases}
$$

Important properties:

* Support: $[0, 1]$ (continuous, not just $\{0, 1\}$).
* Reduces to $\mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$ at $\lambda = 1/2$.
* At $\lambda \to 0$ or $\lambda \to 1$ the density
  concentrates near 0 or 1 respectively.
* Reparameterisable via inverse CDF.

The Continuous Bernoulli is the **correct** likelihood for decoders
that output a value in $[0, 1]$ (e.g. MNIST pixel intensities)
when the model is to remain a valid probabilistic model.  Using a
plain Bernoulli on continuous data produces an *improper* density and
a systematically biased ELBO.

members.signature: Gumbel(loc: Tensor | float, scale: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(loc: Tensor | float, scale: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Gumbel distribution.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description:
Location parameter $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$.  Shifts the
distribution along the real line.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Scale parameter $s > 0$.  Stretches the distribution.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: `μ + s · γ` where `γ` is the Euler–Mascheroni constant.

methods.name: mode
methods.summary: Mode of the Gumbel distribution.

returns.description:
The location parameter $\mu$, which coincides with the mode
of the Gumbel PDF.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Reparameterised sample via the Gumbel icdf: `μ − s · log(−log U)`.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the Gumbel distribution.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Point(s) $x \in \mathbb{R}$ at which to evaluate the density.

returns.description:
Log-density $\log p(x) = -(z + e^{-z}) - \log s$
where $z = (x - \mu) / s$.

members.summary: Gumbel (Type-I extreme value) distribution on $\mathbb{R}$.
members.extended:
Continuous distribution that models the maximum (or minimum) of a
sample drawn from a number of distributions with exponential-like
tails.  It is the limiting distribution of the maximum of IID
exponential-tailed variables (Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko theorem) and is
the basis of the celebrated **Gumbel-max trick** for differentiable
sampling from categorical distributions.

parameters.name: loc
parameters.description:
Location parameter $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ (mode of the
density).

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Scale parameter $s > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Gumbel
>>> d = Gumbel(loc=0.0, scale=1.0)
>>> d.rsample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.0))
Tensor(-1.0)
notes:
Probability density (with $z = (x - \mu)/s$):



$$
p(x; \mu, s) = \frac{1}{s} \exp\!\bigl(-(z + e^{-z})\bigr)
$$

Cumulative distribution:



$$
F(x; \mu, s) = \exp\!\bigl(-e^{-z}\bigr)
$$

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \mu + s\,\gamma_{\mathrm{E}}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = \frac{\pi^2 s^2}{6}
$$

where $\gamma_{\mathrm{E}} \approx 0.5772$ is the
Euler–Mascheroni constant.  The mode is $\mu$ and the entropy
is $\log s + \gamma_{\mathrm{E}} + 1$.

**Gumbel-softmax trick**: if $g_i \sim \mathrm{Gumbel}(0, 1)$
IID and $\pi_i$ are category probabilities, then
$\arg\max_i (\log \pi_i + g_i)$ is a sample from
$\mathrm{Categorical}(\pi)$.  Replacing the argmax with a
softmax yields a continuous relaxation that supports gradient flow
through discrete choices (Jang et al., 2017; Maddison et al., 2017).

members.name: InverseGamma
members.signature: InverseGamma(concentration: Tensor | float, rate: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(concentration: Tensor | float, rate: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise an Inverse-Gamma distribution.

parameters.name: concentration
parameters.description: Shape parameter $\alpha > 0$.

parameters.name: rate
parameters.description: Rate (inverse-scale) parameter $\beta > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Defined for `concentration > 1`: `β / (α − 1)`.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary:
Defined for `concentration > 2`:
`β² / ((α − 1)² (α − 2))`.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Sample by taking the reciprocal of a `Gamma` sample.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the Inverse-Gamma distribution.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Point(s) $x > 0$ at which to evaluate the density.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: `α + log β + log Γ(α) − (1 + α) · ψ(α)`.

members.summary: Inverse-Gamma distribution on $(0, \infty)$.
members.extended:
Distribution of the reciprocal of a Gamma random variable: if
$Y \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)$ then
$X = 1/Y \sim \mathrm{InverseGamma}(\alpha, \beta)$.  Widely used
as the **conjugate prior** for the variance parameter of a Normal
distribution with known mean, and as a prior on positive scale
parameters more generally.

parameters.name: concentration
parameters.description: Shape parameter $\alpha > 0$.

parameters.name: rate
parameters.description: Rate (inverse-scale) parameter $\beta > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import InverseGamma
>>> d = InverseGamma(concentration=3.0, rate=2.0)
>>> d.mean  # β / (α - 1) = 1.0
Tensor(1.0)
>>> d.sample((4,))
Tensor([...])
notes:
Probability density on $x > 0$:



$$
p(x; \alpha, \beta) =
    \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}
    x^{-\alpha - 1} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\beta}{x}\right)
$$

Moments (when defined):



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{\beta}{\alpha - 1}\;\;(\alpha > 1), \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = \frac{\beta^2}{(\alpha - 1)^2 (\alpha - 2)}
\;\;(\alpha > 2)
$$

Mode: $\beta / (\alpha + 1)$.

**Conjugacy**: with a $\mathrm{Normal}(\mu, \sigma^2)$
likelihood and known mean $\mu$, the posterior over
$\sigma^2$ is again Inverse-Gamma with updated parameters
$\alpha' = \alpha + n/2$,
$\beta' = \beta + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_i (x_i - \mu)^2$.

Because Lucid's `lucid.distributions.Gamma` sampler is
rejection-based, `has_rsample` is `False` here and
`sample` returns detached samples obtained by reciprocating
a Gamma draw.

members.name: Kumaraswamy
members.signature: Kumaraswamy(concentration1: Tensor | float, concentration0: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(concentration1: Tensor | float, concentration0: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Kumaraswamy distribution.

parameters.name: concentration1
parameters.description: First shape parameter $a > 0$ (controls the left tail).

parameters.name: concentration0
parameters.description: Second shape parameter $b > 0$ (controls the right tail).

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: `E[X] = b · B(1 + 1/a, b)` expressed via `lgamma`.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: `Var[X] = E[X²] − E[X]²`  where `E[X²] = b · B(1 + 2/a, b)`.

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary:
Reparameterised sample via the icdf:
`x = (1 − (1 − U)^(1/b))^(1/a)`.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-probability density of the Kumaraswamy distribution.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Point(s) $x \in (0, 1)$ at which to evaluate the density.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Entropy via the Beta-distributed auxiliary variable Y = X^a.
methods.extended:
If X ~ Kumaraswamy(a, b) then Y = X^a ~ Beta(1, b), giving:
  E[log X]       = (1/a)(ψ(1) − ψ(b+1)) = −(1/a)(γ + ψ(b+1))
  E[log(1−X^a)] = ψ(b) − ψ(b+1)         = −1/b

H = −log a − log b − (a−1)·E[log X] − (b−1)·E[log(1−X^a)]
  = −log a − log b + (1 − 1/a)(γ + ψ(b+1)) + (1 − 1/b)

members.summary: Kumaraswamy distribution on $(0, 1)$.
members.extended:
Two-parameter continuous distribution on the unit interval that
mimics the shapes of the `lucid.distributions.Beta`
distribution but has a **closed-form CDF and inverse CDF**.  This
makes it a popular choice in variational autoencoders and normalising
flows where cheap, exact reparameterised sampling is needed and no
special functions are required.

parameters.name: concentration1
parameters.description: First shape parameter $a > 0$.

parameters.name: concentration0
parameters.description: Second shape parameter $b > 0$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Kumaraswamy
>>> d = Kumaraswamy(concentration1=2.0, concentration0=5.0)
>>> d.rsample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.3))
Tensor(...)
notes:
Probability density on $x \in (0, 1)$:



$$
p(x; a, b) = a\, b\, x^{a - 1} (1 - x^a)^{b - 1}
$$

Cumulative distribution (closed form):



$$
F(x; a, b) = 1 - (1 - x^a)^b
$$

Inverse CDF (used by `rsample`):



$$
F^{-1}(u; a, b) = (1 - (1 - u)^{1/b})^{1/a}
$$

Moments are expressible in closed form via the Beta function:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X^n] = b\, B(1 + n/a,\, b)
$$

Special cases:

* $a = 1$ → $\mathrm{Beta}(1, b)$
* $b = 1$ → $\mathrm{Beta}(a, 1)$

Unlike Beta, Kumaraswamy is *not* a member of the exponential family
and lacks an analytic normalising integral for sums.  Its main
advantage is the lack of any reliance on the gamma function in
forward or backward passes.

members.name: Multinomial
members.signature: Multinomial(total_count: Tensor | int = 1, probs: Tensor | None = None, logits: Tensor | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(total_count: Tensor | int = 1, probs: Tensor | None = None, logits: Tensor | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Multinomial distribution.

parameters.name: total_count
parameters.description:
Total number of draws $n \geq 0$.  Must be a scalar.
Default is `1` (reduces to a one-hot Categorical).

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Unnormalised probability vector(s) of shape `(..., K)`.
Normalised to sum to 1 internally.  Mutually exclusive with
`logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Unnormalised log-probability vector(s) of shape `(..., K)`.
Converted to probabilities via softmax.  Mutually exclusive with
`probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: Support of the distribution: non-negative integers.

returns.description:
`nonnegative_integer` — the multinomial event count vector lives
in $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}^K$, subject to the additional
constraint that the counts sum to `total_count`.

methods.name: total_count
methods.summary: Total number of trials $n$ per Multinomial draw.

returns.description:
The integer-valued `n` parameter broadcast over the batch shape.
Each independent Multinomial sums to this many trials across the
`K` categories.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: `n · p` (element-wise).

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: `n · p · (1 − p)` (element-wise).

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: `log C(n; k₁,…,kK) + Σ kᵢ log pᵢ`.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw samples by summing one-hot Categorical draws.

members.summary: Multinomial distribution over $K$ categories.
members.extended:
Multivariate generalisation of the `lucid.distributions.Bernoulli`
/Binomial: the joint distribution of the counts
$(k_1, \ldots, k_K)$ obtained when $n = \text{total\_count}$
independent draws are made from a Categorical with probabilities
$(p_1, \ldots, p_K)$.  The $k_i$ are non-negative integers
summing to $n$.

The event dimension is the last axis of `probs` / `logits`; all
preceding axes form the batch shape.

parameters.name: total_count
parameters.description:
Total number of trials $n \geq 0$.  Default `1` (a
one-hot Categorical sample).

parameters.name: probs
parameters.description:
Unnormalised category probabilities of shape `(..., K)` —
normalised internally to sum to 1 along the last axis.  Mutually
exclusive with `logits`.

parameters.name: logits
parameters.description:
Unnormalised log-probabilities of shape `(..., K)`, converted
via softmax.  Mutually exclusive with `probs`.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Multinomial
>>> d = Multinomial(total_count=10, probs=lucid.tensor([0.2, 0.3, 0.5]))
>>> d.mean  # n * p
Tensor([2., 3., 5.])
>>> d.sample((4,))
Tensor([...])
notes:
Probability mass function (joint over the count vector
$\mathbf{k}$ with $\sum_i k_i = n$):



$$
P(\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{k} \mid n, \mathbf{p}) =
    \binom{n}{k_1, \ldots, k_K} \prod_{i=1}^{K} p_i^{k_i},
\qquad
\binom{n}{k_1, \ldots, k_K} = \frac{n!}{\prod_i k_i!}
$$

Moments (per category):



$$
\mathbb{E}[K_i] = n p_i, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[K_i] = n p_i (1 - p_i), \qquad
\mathrm{Cov}[K_i, K_j] = -n p_i p_j \;\; (i \neq j)
$$

Special cases:

* $K = 2$ → $\mathrm{Binomial}(n, p_1)$
* $n = 1$ → one-hot `lucid.distributions.Categorical`

**Conjugate prior**: `lucid.distributions.Dirichlet` —
observing counts $\mathbf{k}$ updates
`Dirichlet(α) → Dirichlet(α + k)`.

Sampling is non-reparameterised (`has_rsample = False`) and
implemented by summing $n$ one-hot Categorical draws.

members.name: LKJCholesky
members.signature: LKJCholesky(dim: int, concentration: Tensor | float = 1.0, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(dim: int, concentration: Tensor | float = 1.0, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise an LKJ distribution over Cholesky factors.
methods.extended:
Pre-computes the Beta distribution parameters used by the vectorised
Onion sampler (Lewandowski et al. 2009, §3).

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Dimension $D \geq 2$ of the correlation matrix.

parameters.name: concentration
parameters.description:
Shape parameter $\eta > 0$.  Default is `1.0` (uniform
distribution over correlation matrices).  Larger values concentrate
mass near the identity (near-zero correlations).

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: Constraint: positive-definite matrices (proxy for correlation-Cholesky support).

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw a sample via the vectorised Onion method (Lewandowski 2009 §3).
methods.extended: Returns the lower Cholesky factor `L` of a random correlation matrix.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of the LKJ distribution on a Cholesky factor.
methods.extended:
The density is proportional to
`∏_{i=2}^{d} L_{ii}^{order_i}`
where `order_i = 2(η − 1) + (d − i)`.

The normaliser follows Eq. (15) of Lewandowski et al. (2009).

members.summary: LKJ distribution over Cholesky factors of correlation matrices.
members.extended:
Generative prior for the lower-triangular Cholesky factor $L$
of a correlation matrix $R = L L^\top$ of size $D \times D$.
Density is proportional to $\det(R)^{\eta - 1}$ so the
$\eta$ parameter ("concentration") controls how concentrated
the prior is around the identity (uncorrelated).  Widely used as a
weakly-informative prior on correlation structure in Bayesian
hierarchical models — replacing an inverse-Wishart prior (which
couples scale and correlation) with a clean separation.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Size $D$ of the correlation matrix (must be $\geq 2$).

parameters.name: concentration
parameters.description:
Shape parameter $\eta > 0$.  Default `1.0` (uniform over
the space of correlation matrices).  $\eta > 1$ concentrates
mass near the identity (low correlation), $\eta < 1$
favours high-correlation factors.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import LKJCholesky
>>> dist = LKJCholesky(dim=4, concentration=2.0)
>>> L = dist.sample()           # (4, 4) lower-triangular Cholesky factor
>>> R = L @ L.T                 # implied correlation matrix
>>> R.diagonal()                # diagonal of R is all 1's
Tensor([1., 1., 1., 1.])
Use as a Bayesian prior over correlation structure:
>>> prior = LKJCholesky(dim=8, concentration=1.5)
>>> # ... pair with marginal scales / standard deviations to form a
>>> #     covariance prior: Σ = diag(σ) · R · diag(σ)
notes:
Probability density on the manifold of valid Cholesky factors
$L$:



$$
p(L; \eta) \propto |\det R(L)|^{\eta - 1}
= \prod_{i=1}^{D-1} L_{ii}^{D - i + 2(\eta - 1)}
$$

where the second equality follows from the Jacobian of the
transformation between $R$ and its Cholesky factor.

**Sampling** uses the vectorised **Onion method** (Lewandowski,
Kurowicka & Joe, 2009, §3) — row $i$ is built incrementally so
that its squared norm equals one, with off-diagonal magnitude drawn
from a Beta and angular components from a uniform on the sphere.
The full sampler runs in $\mathcal{O}(D^2)$ per draw with no
rejections.

For $\eta = 1$ the distribution is uniform over valid
correlation matrices (specifically, over the set of valid Cholesky
factors).

members.name: Wishart
members.signature: Wishart(df: Tensor | float, covariance_matrix: Tensor | None = None, precision_matrix: Tensor | None = None, scale_tril: Tensor | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(df: Tensor | float, covariance_matrix: Tensor | None = None, precision_matrix: Tensor | None = None, scale_tril: Tensor | None = None, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Initialise a Wishart distribution.
methods.extended:
Converts `covariance_matrix` or `precision_matrix` to a Cholesky
factor internally.  Exactly one scale specification must be provided.

parameters.name: df
parameters.description:
Degrees of freedom $\nu > D - 1$ where $D$ is the
matrix dimension.  Values $\nu \leq D - 1$ violate the
positive-definiteness guarantee.

parameters.name: covariance_matrix
parameters.description:
Positive-definite scale matrix $\Sigma$ of shape
`(..., D, D)`.

parameters.name: precision_matrix
parameters.description:
Positive-definite precision matrix $\Sigma^{-1}$ of shape
`(..., D, D)`.  Converted via $\Sigma = (\Sigma^{-1})^{-1}$.

parameters.name: scale_tril
parameters.description:
Lower-triangular Cholesky factor $L$ of $\Sigma$,
shape `(..., D, D)` with positive diagonal.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: Constraint: positive-definite symmetric matrices.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: `df · Σ`.

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: `Var[W_{ij}] = df · (Σ_{ij}² + Σ_{ii} Σ_{jj})`.

methods.name: sample
methods.summary: Draw a sample via the Bartlett decomposition.
methods.extended:
Each sample is `L A Aᵀ Lᵀ` where `L = scale_tril` and `A`
is a random lower-triangular matrix with:

* diagonal `A_{ii} ~ sqrt(χ²_{df − i})`  for `i = 0, …, d−1`,
* off-diagonal `A_{ij} ~ N(0, 1)`  for `i > j`.

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of the Wishart distribution.
methods.extended:
```
log p(X) = (df − d − 1)/2 · log|X|
         − tr(Σ⁻¹ X) / 2
         − df · d / 2 · log 2
         − df/2 · log|Σ|
         − log Γ_d(df/2)
```

where `Γ_d` is the multivariate gamma function.

members.summary: Wishart distribution over symmetric positive-definite matrices.
members.extended:
Matrix-variate generalisation of the
`lucid.distributions.Chi2` / Gamma distribution: the
distribution of the (unnormalised) sample covariance matrix
$S = \sum_{i=1}^{\nu} \mathbf{z}_i \mathbf{z}_i^\top$ formed
from $\nu$ independent Multivariate Normal draws
$\mathbf{z}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \Sigma)$.  It is the
**conjugate prior** of the precision matrix of a Multivariate Normal
with known mean.

Specify exactly one of `covariance_matrix`, `precision_matrix`, or
`scale_tril` — the other parameterisations are derived internally
via Cholesky factorisation.

parameters.name: df
parameters.description:
Degrees of freedom $\nu > D - 1$ where $D$ is the
matrix dimension.  Values $\nu \leq D - 1$ violate the
positive-definiteness guarantee.

parameters.name: covariance_matrix
parameters.description:
Positive-definite scale matrix $\Sigma$ of shape
`(..., D, D)`.

parameters.name: precision_matrix
parameters.description:
Positive-definite precision matrix $\Sigma^{-1}$ of shape
`(..., D, D)`.

parameters.name: scale_tril
parameters.description:
Lower-triangular Cholesky factor $L$ of $\Sigma$
(so $\Sigma = L L^\top$), shape `(..., D, D)` with
positive diagonal.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Wishart
>>> Sigma = lucid.tensor([[2.0, 0.5], [0.5, 1.0]])
>>> d = Wishart(df=5.0, covariance_matrix=Sigma)
>>> d.sample((4,))
Tensor([...])
notes:
Probability density on the cone of symmetric positive-definite
$D \times D$ matrices:



$$
p(\mathbf{X}; \nu, \Sigma) =
    \frac{|\mathbf{X}|^{(\nu - D - 1)/2}
          \exp\!\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{tr}(\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{X})\bigr)}
         {2^{\nu D / 2} |\Sigma|^{\nu/2} \Gamma_D(\nu/2)}
$$

where $\Gamma_D$ is the multivariate gamma function:



$$
\Gamma_D(a) = \pi^{D(D-1)/4} \prod_{i=1}^{D} \Gamma\!\left(a + \tfrac{1 - i}{2}\right)
$$

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}] = \nu \Sigma, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X_{ij}] =
    \nu (\Sigma_{ij}^2 + \Sigma_{ii} \Sigma_{jj})
$$

Special cases / relations:

* $D = 1$ → $\mathrm{Wishart}(\nu, \sigma^2) = \sigma^2 \chi^2(\nu)$.
* Inverse: if $\mathbf{X} \sim \mathrm{Wishart}(\nu, \Sigma)$
  then $\mathbf{X}^{-1}$ follows the inverse-Wishart
  distribution, the conjugate prior of the **covariance** (not
  precision) matrix.
* Bartlett decomposition: $\mathbf{X} = L A A^\top L^\top$
  with $A$ lower-triangular containing $\chi^2$ draws on
  the diagonal and standard Normals below — used for sampling.

members.name: Uniform
members.signature: Uniform(low: Tensor | float, high: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(low: Tensor | float, high: Tensor | float, validate_args: bool | None = None)
methods.summary: Construct a Uniform distribution on `[low, high)`.

parameters.name: low
parameters.description: Lower bound $a \in \mathbb{R}$.

parameters.name: high
parameters.description: Upper bound $b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $b > a$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> from lucid.distributions import Uniform
>>> d = Uniform(low=0.0, high=1.0)
>>> d.mean
Tensor(0.5)
notes:
The Uniform distribution has constant PDF on its support:



$$
p(x; a, b) = \frac{1}{b - a}, \quad x \in [a, b)
$$

All values in the interval are equally likely.  The distribution is
the maximum-entropy distribution over a bounded interval, and is
widely used for non-informative priors and Monte-Carlo sampling via
the inverse-CDF method.

methods.name: support
methods.summary: The support constraint of the Uniform distribution.
methods.extended:
Returns the `real` constraint as a conservative fallback, because
the bounds may be arbitrary tensors and a precise interval constraint
would require tensor-aware bound tracking.

methods.name: mean
methods.summary: Expected value of the Uniform distribution.
examples:
>>> Uniform(low=2.0, high=8.0).mean
Tensor(5.0)

methods.name: variance
methods.summary: Variance of the Uniform distribution.
examples:
>>> Uniform(low=0.0, high=1.0).variance  # 1/12 ≈ 0.0833
Tensor(0.0833)

methods.name: rsample
methods.summary: Reparameterised sample using the location-scale transform.
methods.extended:
$$
X = a + (b - a) \cdot U, \quad U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)
$$

Gradients flow through both $a$ and $b$.

parameters.name: sample_shape
parameters.description: Leading shape dimensions for the sample batch.  Default is `()`.

returns.description: Samples in $[a, b)$ of shape `sample_shape + batch_shape`.

examples:
>>> d = Uniform(low=-1.0, high=1.0)
>>> x = d.rsample((100,))
>>> (x >= -1.0).all() and (x < 1.0).all()
True

methods.name: log_prob
methods.summary: Log-density of `value` under the Uniform distribution.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Values at which to evaluate the log-density.

examples:
>>> Uniform(low=0.0, high=2.0).log_prob(lucid.tensor(1.0))
Tensor(-0.6931)

methods.name: cdf
methods.summary: Cumulative distribution function of the Uniform distribution.

methods.name: entropy
methods.summary: Shannon entropy of the Uniform distribution (in nats).
methods.extended:
$$
H(X) = \log(b - a)
$$

The Uniform distribution maximises entropy among all distributions
supported on a bounded interval of fixed length.
examples:
>>> Uniform(low=0.0, high=1.0).entropy()  # log(1) = 0.0
Tensor(0.0)

members.summary: Continuous Uniform distribution on the half-open interval $[a, b)$.
members.extended:
Maximum-entropy distribution over a bounded interval of fixed length:
every point in $[a, b)$ is equally likely.  Widely used as a
non-informative prior, as the source of randomness for inverse-CDF
sampling of arbitrary distributions, and as a quasi-Monte-Carlo
integration measure.

parameters.name: low
parameters.description: Lower bound $a \in \mathbb{R}$.

parameters.name: high
parameters.description: Upper bound $b \in \mathbb{R}$ with $b > a$.

parameters.name: validate_args
parameters.description: If `True`, validate parameter constraints at construction time.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.distributions import Uniform
>>> d = Uniform(low=0.0, high=1.0)
>>> d.mean
Tensor(0.5)
>>> d.rsample((4,))
Tensor([...])
>>> d.log_prob(lucid.tensor(0.5))
Tensor(0.0)
notes:
Probability density (constant on the support):



$$
p(x; a, b) = \begin{cases}
    \dfrac{1}{b - a} & x \in [a, b) \\[2pt]
    0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Cumulative distribution:



$$
F(x; a, b) = \mathrm{clip}\!\left(\frac{x - a}{b - a},\; 0,\; 1\right)
$$

Moments:



$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{a + b}{2}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}[X] = \frac{(b - a)^2}{12}, \qquad
H[X] = \log(b - a)
$$

Higher moments (centred at the midpoint $m = (a+b)/2$):
$\mathbb{E}[(X-m)^{2k}] = (b-a)^{2k}/(2k+1)\,2^{2k}$; odd
central moments are zero by symmetry.

The Uniform is the **maximum-entropy distribution** over a bounded
interval of fixed length, and it underpins the inverse-CDF
(Smirnov) sampling identity: if $U \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)$
and $F^{-1}$ is the quantile function of any 1-D distribution,
then $F^{-1}(U)$ has that distribution.

Reparameterised sampling uses the location-scale transform
$X = a + (b - a) U$ so gradients flow through both endpoints.

summary:
`lucid.distributions` — probability distributions with the
reference-framework's interface (sample / rsample / log_prob / entropy / …).
extended:
Mirrors the reference framework's distributions surface for the most-used univariate and
discrete families plus `MultivariateNormal`, `Beta`, `Gamma`, and
`Dirichlet` from the gamma family.  See [[api-python-distributions]]
for the full surface.
